articles.content:
Britain is set to have its seventh prime minister in 10 years after Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced his resignation on June 22, 2026, a decade after the country voted to leave the European Union. The change extends a period of rapid turnover in British leadership that began after the 2016 Brexit referendum.

Starmer took office in July 2024 after leading Labour to a landslide election victory. His government later faced criticism over policy reversals, weak economic conditions and the rise of Reform UK, led by Nigel Farage.

Before Starmer, Rishi Sunak served from October 2022 to July 2024. He took office after the market turmoil that followed Liz Truss’s premiership and focused on restoring economic stability, but his Conservative Party lost power after 14 years in government.

Truss served for 49 days from September to October 2022. Her government’s unfunded tax-cutting plans triggered financial instability, a falling pound and a sharp loss of market confidence.

Boris Johnson was prime minister from July 2019 to September 2022. He won a large parliamentary majority after campaigning on a pledge to complete Britain’s exit from the European Union. His premiership later became defined in part by the “Partygate” scandal, involving reports of gatherings inside government during COVID-19 restrictions.

Theresa May served from July 2016 to July 2019 after David Cameron resigned following the Brexit referendum. May attempted to negotiate terms for Britain’s withdrawal from the European Union while facing opposition from both hard-line Brexit supporters and lawmakers concerned about economic damage. Her authority weakened after she lost her parliamentary majority in the 2017 snap election.

Cameron, who served from May 2010 to July 2016, called the Brexit referendum in an effort to settle divisions within the Conservative Party. Voters chose to leave the European Union by 52 percent to 48 percent, and he resigned the next morning.

The article also notes the continued presence of Larry the Cat, the official chief mouser at Downing Street since 2011, as a recurring figure during the turnover in British leadership.

articles.headline: AP review says public evidence does not support Netanyahu claim that Iran was on verge of using nuclear weapon

articles.summary:
An Associated Press fact check published Monday said there is no public evidence supporting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's claim that Iran would have developed and used a nuclear weapon against Israel if not for two recent wars. The article said that assessment differs from public findings by the International Atomic Energy Agency and U.S. intelligence agencies.

The report said Iran has enriched uranium to 60 percent purity, which is close to weapons grade but below the roughly 90
articles.content:
An Associated Press fact check published Monday said there is no public evidence supporting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's claim that Iran would have developed and used a nuclear weapon against Israel if not for two recent wars. The article said that assessment differs from public findings by the International Atomic Energy Agency and U.S. intelligence agencies.

The report said Iran has enriched uranium to 60 percent purity, which is close to weapons grade but below the roughly 90 percent generally required for a usable nuclear weapon. It cited IAEA Director-General Rafael Mariano Grossi as saying Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium could potentially be enough for as many as 10 bombs if Iran chose to build them. The article also said there has been no public evidence of an active Iranian nuclear weapons program since 2003.

According to the AP, a 2025 assessment from the U.S. Office of the Director of National Intelligence said, "We continue to assess Iran is not building a nuclear weapon." The report said that even before recent military action, Iran was still months or years away from producing a usable atomic weapon because it would need to further enrich uranium, build a bomb and likely miniaturize it for missile delivery.

The article said Israeli and U.S. strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities in 2025 and 2026 damaged centrifuges and disrupted enrichment, while Iran has blocked IAEA visits to bombed sites. It also noted that some Iranian officials have suggested the country should seek nuclear weapons if its existence is threatened, while Iranian authorities have long maintained that the nuclear program is for peaceful purposes.

France 24 published a video profile of France men's national football coach Didier Deschamps ahead of the 2026 World Cup, which is expected to be his last tournament in charge of the team. Vice President JD Vance said Monday that Iran had agreed to allow international inspections of its nuclear program as U.S. and Iranian officials continued talks in Switzerland on turning a recent ceasefire into a broader agreement. Vance said technical negotiations were continuing with mediation from Qatar and Pakistan.

According to Vance, the talks cover issues including Iran's uranium stockpile, access for inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency and security in the Strait of Ho
articles.content:
Vice President JD Vance said Monday that Iran had agreed to allow international inspections of its nuclear program as U.S. and Iranian officials continued talks in Switzerland on turning a recent ceasefire into a broader agreement. Vance said technical negotiations were continuing with mediation from Qatar and Pakistan.

According to Vance, the talks cover issues including Iran's uranium stockpile, access for inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency and security in the Strait of Hormuz. He said conversations with IAEA inspectors could begin as soon as Monday, while acknowledging that negotiators still needed to settle where inspectors would be allowed to go and what activities they could verify. Iran Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said interaction with the IAEA would continue in line with decisions by Iran's legislature and Supreme National Security Council.

The Washington Post article said the ceasefire memorandum signed Wednesday gave the United States and Iran 60 days to resolve major disputes. Over the weekend, tensions continued as Israeli attacks in Lebanon tested the arrangement and Iran threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz. Vance said the strait remained open and that negotiators were discussing a mechanism to keep it that way. The U.S. Treasury Department also waived sanctions on Iranian oil for the 60-day ceasefire period, according to the report.

The report said U.S. and Iranian officials have offered different public interpretations of when frozen Iranian assets could be accessed. Vance said any release would depend on continued progress and could be limited to purchases such as American food and other exports. Two U.S. officials cited by the newspaper said American force levels in the region had not yet been reduced.

articles.headline: Keir Starmer announces resignation, triggering Labour leadership contest
articles.deck: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Monday that he will step down, setting off a Labour Party leadership contest expected to conclude before Parliament returns in September.
articles.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Monday that he will step down, setting off a Labour Party leadership contest expected to conclude before Parliament returns in September. Speaking outside 10 Downing Street, Starmer said he had informed King Charles of his decision and would remain in office until a successor is chosen.

Starmer said Labour's National Executive Committee would open nominations on July 9 and aim to complete the process by the summer recess on July 16, or shortly after if a
articles.content:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Monday that he will step down, setting off a Labour Party leadership contest expected to conclude before Parliament returns in September. Speaking outside 10 Downing Street, Starmer said he had informed King Charles of his decision and would remain in office until a successor is chosen.

Starmer said Labour's National Executive Committee would open nominations on July 9 and aim to complete the process by the summer recess on July 16, or shortly after if a contest is required. Under party rules, candidates need support from 20 percent of Labour MPs to qualify. With Labour holding 403 seats in Parliament, that threshold is 81 MPs.

The resignation follows pressure within Labour after local election setbacks in May and growing concern among party lawmakers about the rise of Reform UK. The article says some Labour critics have also linked declining support among parts of the party's base to Starmer's positions on immigration, Israel and welfare cuts.

Former Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham was described as the leading contender after returning to Parliament in a by-election in Makerfield. Former Health Secretary Wes Streeting and former Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner were also mentioned as possible contenders, but both indicated support for Burnham. If more than one candidate qualifies, Labour members would vote using a preferential ballot. Once a new leader is selected, Starmer would formally resign and the king would invite the new Labour leader to form a government.

Voronezh Governor Alexander Gusev said a production plant was damaged and three people were injured, but he did not identify the site. Moscow May
articles.content:
Ukraine's military said Monday that it struck a plant producing missile electronics in Russia's Voronezh region and the Dubna satellite communications center in the Moscow region. In a statement on Telegram, the Ukrainian General Staff said it used air-launched cruise missiles and described the Voronezh facility as part of Russia's defense production.

Voronezh Governor Alexander Gusev said a production plant was damaged and three people were injured, but he did not identify the site. Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said 84 drones headed for the capital were shot down in the previous 24 hours. Russian news agencies, citing the Defense Ministry, reported that air defense systems downed 301 drones overnight, including in Russian-occupied areas of Ukraine. Flight operations were temporarily suspended at four airports serving Moscow, according to Russia's aviation watchdog.

In Ukraine, officials said a Russian drone attack in the Sumy region killed a 13-year-old boy, his 36-year-old father and his 73-year-old grandmother. Ukraine's emergency services said drone attacks in Zaporizhzhia killed two people and injured seven others. Odesa regional Governor Oleh Kiper said a Russian Iskander missile strike on an agricultural facility Sunday evening killed one person and injured three.

Ukraine's navy said Russian drones hit the Turkish dry cargo vessel Victress, sailing under the Panamanian flag. Deputy Prime Minister Oleksii Kuleba said a 58-year-old Egyptian cook was killed and eight crew members, including Turkish and Indian nationals, evacuated by lifeboat. Separately, officials said European military leaders from Germany, France, Britain, Italy and Poland were due to meet in Berlin on Wednesday to discuss the war in Ukraine and the upcoming NATO summit.

articles.headline: Study Finds Heat Stress Seasons Have Lengthened Across Many Regions Since the 1970s

articles.summary:
A study published Monday in Nature Climate Change found that many regions around the world now experience substantially more days of heat stress than they did in the 1970s.

The research examined “feels-like” temperatures using the Universal Thermal Climate Index, which combines temperature, humidity, wind speed and other factors to estimate how heat affects the human body. articles.content:
A study published Monday in Nature Climate Change found that many regions around the world now experience substantially more days of heat stress than they did in the 1970s.

The research examined “feels-like” temperatures using the Universal Thermal Climate Index, which combines temperature, humidity, wind speed and other factors to estimate how heat affects the human body. The study found increases in strong, very strong and extreme heat stress, as well as warmer nights that can limit recovery from daytime heat.

According to the report, parts of southern Africa, eastern Africa, Mexico and Central America may now experience around 50 more days each year of at least strong heat stress than in the 1970s. Parts of southern Europe, including areas of Spain, Italy, Greece and Turkey, have also seen major increases.

The study’s lead author, Rebecca Emerton of the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts, said the findings show heat stress intensifying both in already hot regions and in places where it was previously rare. The article said about one billion more people now face at least one day of extreme heat stress each year compared with the 1970s.

Researchers and outside experts said the results underscore the need for emissions reductions, heat health action plans, early warning systems and adaptation measures.

articles.content:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio is scheduled to travel this week to the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain for meetings with Gulf allies as the Trump administration continues negotiations with Iran.

Rubio is also expected to meet representatives of the Gulf Cooperation Council in Bahrain. The trip follows Vice President JD Vance’s meeting with Iranian officials in Switzerland as part of a 60-day effort to build on the ceasefire framework announced last week.

The Washington Post reported that the talks with Iran have drawn attention in Washington because Rubio, who has long taken a hard line on Iran and opposed the 2015 nuclear agreement, has played a less visible public role than Vance.

Critics of the current framework said the proposed arrangement would give Iran sanctions relief and other economic benefits in exchange for steps including giving up highly enriched uranium. State Department spokesperson Tommy Pigott said Rubio and the administration were fully aligned behind President Donald Trump.

Analysts said Rubio’s meetings in the Gulf are likely to focus on reassuring regional partners affected by Iranian strikes and economic disruption tied to recent tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.

President Donald Trump said the site had been vandalized and that the damage included chemicals being introduced into the pool, but the report said he did not provide evidence for that claim. The National Park Service has been treating the water since June 16 with hydrogen peroxide, while the Interior Department said nanobubble ozone techn
articles.content:
The Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool in Washington, DC, has developed a significant algae bloom weeks after a $14.7 million renovation project was completed.

President Donald Trump said the site had been vandalized and that the damage included chemicals being introduced into the pool, but the report said he did not provide evidence for that claim. The National Park Service has been treating the water since June 16 with hydrogen peroxide, while the Interior Department said nanobubble ozone technology was also being used.

The report said the renovation included repainting the bottom of the pool blue, but the coating began peeling and parts of it were later seen floating on the surface. Experts interviewed by US media said algae blooms are common at this time of year and noted that shallow, stagnant water and strong sunlight create conditions that support algae growth.

One man was detained after touching peeling material in the pool, according to the Associated Press, but said he had stopped only to examine the damaged coating. Investigations are continuing.

The article also reviewed scrutiny over no-bid contracts tied to the renovation and related water treatment work, as well as other renovation and construction projects announced by Trump in Washington.

Vice President JD Vance said talks in Switzerland had crea
articles.content:
The United States has issued a 60-day waiver easing some sanctions on Iranian oil exports following recent talks between Washington and Tehran.

According to the report, the US Treasury action allows the production, delivery and sale of Iranian crude oil, petrochemical products and petroleum products through August 21. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the step followed progress under a memorandum of understanding signed on June 17.

Vice President JD Vance said talks in Switzerland had created a basis for a broader agreement. Mediators cited by Reuters also described the discussions as encouraging.

Bessent said Iran had committed to allowing International Atomic Energy Agency inspectors into the country and to maintaining open transit in the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian officials did not immediately respond, according to the report.

Oil prices fell after the waiver was announced, with Brent crude down more than 3.5 percent to $77.7 a barrel. The article also said ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz had increased, though crossings remained below levels seen before recent hostilities.

During the campaign, De la Espriella said he would pursue a hardline security agenda, reduce the size of the state and issue 90 executive decrees on his first day in office. He also said he would support fossil fuel extraction a
articles.content:
Abelardo de la Espriella, a lawyer and businessman with no previous experience in elected office, has been elected president of Colombia after defeating Senator Iván Cepeda by about 250,000 votes. He is due to take office on August 7, succeeding President Gustavo Petro.

During the campaign, De la Espriella said he would pursue a hardline security agenda, reduce the size of the state and issue 90 executive decrees on his first day in office. He also said he would support fossil fuel extraction and fracking, loosen some environmental licensing rules, and consider major changes to Colombia’s international commitments.

His victory speech included a pledge to respect the constitution and the rights of all Colombians. At the same time, activists, legal advocates and analysts interviewed by The Guardian said they were concerned about the possible impact of his agenda on democratic institutions, civil rights and freedom of expression.

De la Espriella previously built his profile as a lawyer representing high-profile clients, including paramilitary leaders and other prominent defendants. He later expanded into a range of business ventures and became a visible public figure on social media.

The report said he has been compared by supporters and critics to other right-wing populist leaders in the region and abroad. Analysts quoted in the article said his plans could face legal challenges, but they also warned that policy moves made early in his term could have lasting consequences before courts rule on them.

Burnham, 56, recently returned to Parliament after winning a by-election in Makerfield and is widely expected to seek the leadership.

Burnham served for nine years as mayor of Greater Manchester, where he built a national profile through policies on transport, housing and economic development. Supporters point to g
articles.content:
Andy Burnham has emerged as the leading candidate to become the next leader of Britain’s governing Labour Party after Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced his resignation on Monday. Burnham, 56, recently returned to Parliament after winning a by-election in Makerfield and is widely expected to seek the leadership.

Burnham served for nine years as mayor of Greater Manchester, where he built a national profile through policies on transport, housing and economic development. Supporters point to growth in Manchester and his decision to bring the region’s bus network under public control as evidence of a mix of pro-business and interventionist policies.

Before becoming mayor, Burnham spent 16 years in Parliament and served in Gordon Brown’s cabinet as culture secretary and later health secretary. He previously ran for Labour leader in 2010 and 2015.

Supporters say Burnham could help Labour regain support in areas where the party has struggled, including pro-Brexit constituencies. Critics say he has at times adjusted his public positions to fit changing political conditions.

Burnham was born in Aintree, near Liverpool, and grew up in Culcheth. He joined Labour as a teenager and studied English at Fitzwilliam College, Cambridge. He is married to Marie-France Van Heel and they have three children.

Analysts say that while Burnham has a strong domestic record, he has limited experience in foreign policy and national security. If elected Labour leader, he could take office as prime minister as soon as mid-July.

Community groups in Maiduguri, northeastern Nigeria, are using women-led mediation and dialogue programs to try to reduce youth gang violence that has affected neighborhoods in the city and nearby Jere.

Residents and community leaders told Al Jazeera that gangs known locally as Marlians have carried out attacks, robberies and territorial clashes for years. In 2023, Borno State Governor Babagana Umara Zulum ordered a crackdown after a series of deadly incidents.

articles.content:
Community groups in Maiduguri, northeastern Nigeria, are using women-led mediation and dialogue programs to try to reduce youth gang violence that has affected neighborhoods in the city and nearby Jere.

Residents and community leaders told Al Jazeera that gangs known locally as Marlians have carried out attacks, robberies and territorial clashes for years. In 2023, Borno State Governor Babagana Umara Zulum ordered a crackdown after a series of deadly incidents.

Local organizations including Unified Members for Women Advancement, the Ajilari Cross Development Association and the Gomari Development Association said they have held regular dialogue sessions with gang leaders and former members since 2018. They said the goal is to persuade young men to leave violence and help resolve disputes before they escalate.

Community leaders estimate that more than 1,000 gang members have taken part in the dialogue circles, although that figure could not be independently verified. Former gang members interviewed by Al Jazeera said the process helped them reconsider their actions, but some also said they still face threats from rivals.

Organizers said the progress remains fragile because there is no formal reintegration system and donor funding for mediation efforts has declined.

Marcus Holmgren Pedersen gave Norway the lead late in the first half after a defensive error, and Erling Haaland added a second goal shortly after the break. Ismaila Sarr cut the deficit for Senegal, but Haaland scored again to restore Norway’s two-goal advantage.

articles.content:
Norway defeated Senegal 3-2 on Monday to secure a place in the World Cup knockout stage with one group match remaining.

Marcus Holmgren Pedersen gave Norway the lead late in the first half after a defensive error, and Erling Haaland added a second goal shortly after the break. Ismaila Sarr cut the deficit for Senegal, but Haaland scored again to restore Norway’s two-goal advantage.

Sarr scored a second late goal for Senegal, but Norway held on for the win. Haaland has scored four goals in two matches at the tournament.

Norway will face France on Friday to decide the top spot in the group. Senegal must beat Iraq in its final match and rely on other results to keep its hopes of advancing alive.

articles.headline: Keir Starmer’s premiership unraveled after election victory, policy reversals and party losses
articles.deck: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned less than two years after leading Labour to a large election victory in July 2024, following pressure from within his party after he...
articles.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned less than two years after leading Labour to a large election victory in July 2024, following pressure from within his party after heavy local election losses.

Labour won 411 of 650 seats in Parliament in the 2024 general election, ending 14 years of Conservative rule. But Starmer’s government soon faced weak economic growth, criticism over tax and spending decisions, and a series of policy reversals that opponents and some Labour lawmakers said damag
articles.content:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned less than two years after leading Labour to a large election victory in July 2024, following pressure from within his party after heavy local election losses.

Labour won 411 of 650 seats in Parliament in the 2024 general election, ending 14 years of Conservative rule. But Starmer’s government soon faced weak economic growth, criticism over tax and spending decisions, and a series of policy reversals that opponents and some Labour lawmakers said damaged his authority.

The Associated Press reported that Labour’s decision to increase a payroll-related tax on businesses was unpopular and that the government later reversed or softened other measures, including changes affecting winter heating subsidies, welfare spending and agricultural inheritance rules.

Starmer also faced scrutiny over his appointment of Peter Mandelson as UK ambassador to the United States. Mandelson was later removed after renewed questions about his past ties to Jeffrey Epstein and reports about security and reputational concerns. Mandelson has denied wrongdoing.

After Labour lost ground in local elections and more than 100 Labour members of Parliament called on Starmer to step down, political pressure intensified. Starmer announced his resignation Monday, and Andy Burnham was sworn in later that day in the House of Commons after returning to Parliament in a by-election.

articles.headline: Analysis says Brexit continues to weigh on UK economy 10 years after vote
articles.deck: Economists interviewed by France 24 said the United Kingdom’s decision to leave the European Union continues to reduce economic output a decade after the 2016 referendum.
articles.summary:
Economists interviewed by France 24 said the United Kingdom’s decision to leave the European Union continues to reduce economic output a decade after the 2016 referendum.

Researchers cited in the report estimated the UK economy is between 6 percent and 8 percent smaller than it would have been without Brexit, based on comparisons with similar countries and with firms that trade heavily with the EU. Jonathan Portes of King’s College London said the reduced output also means lower tax revenue for
articles.content:
Economists interviewed by France 24 said the United Kingdom’s decision to leave the European Union continues to reduce economic output a decade after the 2016 referendum.

Researchers cited in the report estimated the UK economy is between 6 percent and 8 percent smaller than it would have been without Brexit, based on comparisons with similar countries and with firms that trade heavily with the EU. Jonathan Portes of King’s College London said the reduced output also means lower tax revenue for public services.

The report said trade in goods, particularly cars and agricultural products, has been affected by additional paperwork and border requirements. Britain’s Food and Drink Federation said UK food exports to the EU have fallen by nearly a quarter since Brexit took effect.

Economists also linked Brexit to higher costs for some consumers, though they said it is difficult to isolate Brexit from other pressures including the COVID-19 pandemic, wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, and longer-term issues such as weak investment and slow growth.

Polling cited by France 24 showed growing public regret over Brexit, while analysts said any effort to reduce the economic impact would likely depend on negotiating closer arrangements with the EU.

A series of developments in Gaza and the occupied West Bank this week drew renewed international criticism, including Israeli moves affecting planning authority in Hebron, military activity in Gaza and settler-related violence in the West Bank.

articles.content:
A series of developments in Gaza and the occupied West Bank this week drew renewed international criticism, including Israeli moves affecting planning authority in Hebron, military activity in Gaza and settler-related violence in the West Bank.

In Hebron, Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel had canceled the 1997 Hebron agreement and taken planning authority in the H2 sector, which includes Israeli settlements and the Ibrahimi Mosque. Israel’s Foreign Ministry later said the agreement itself had not been canceled but that planning powers had been transferred by a previous cabinet decision. The Palestinian Authority and the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation condemned the move, and the US State Department said it does not support annexation of the West Bank.

Al Jazeera reported that Israeli media described a gradual westward expansion of Israeli-controlled lines in Gaza. The Palestinian Centre for Human Rights estimated Israeli forces now control about 64 percent of the territory. Those figures could not be independently verified.

The article also reported that Gaza’s Health Ministry said more than 1,000 people have been killed since a ceasefire took effect and that an Israeli strike in al-Bureij killed Al Jazeera Mubasher cameraman Ahmad Wishah. Israel disputed Al Jazeera’s account and said, without publicly providing evidence in the article, that Wishah was a Hamas operative.

In the West Bank, Palestinian media and activists reported mosque arson attacks, damage to water infrastructure and additional settlement construction. Israeli authorities confirmed some incidents and, in one case, carried out demolitions at settler outposts. International organizations and several governments continued to criticize settlement expansion and humanitarian conditions in Gaza.

articles.headline: Algeria rallies past Jordan for first World Cup win since 2014
articles.deck: Algeria came from behind to beat Jordan 2-1 on Monday night, recording its first World Cup victory since 2014 and improving its chances of reaching the knockout stage.
articles.summary:
Algeria came from behind to beat Jordan 2-1 on Monday night, recording its first World Cup victory since 2014 and improving its chances of reaching the knockout stage.

Jordan took the lead in the first half through Nizar Al Rashdan, the country’s first World Cup goal. Algeria equalized in the 69th minute when substitute Nadhir Benbouali headed in a corner from Riyad Mahrez.

Amine Gouiri scored the winner in the 82nd minute, getting a touch in the box that deflected off goalkeeper Yazeed Abulai
articles.content:
Algeria came from behind to beat Jordan 2-1 on Monday night, recording its first World Cup victory since 2014 and improving its chances of reaching the knockout stage.

Jordan took the lead in the first half through Nizar Al Rashdan, the country’s first World Cup goal. Algeria equalized in the 69th minute when substitute Nadhir Benbouali headed in a corner from Riyad Mahrez.

Amine Gouiri scored the winner in the 82nd minute, getting a touch in the box that deflected off goalkeeper Yazeed Abulaila and into the net.

The result leaves Algeria in position to advance from Group J with a win against Austria on Saturday. Jordan was eliminated after losing its first two matches in its first World Cup appearance.

articles.headline: Polish activist’s death in Ecuador prompts calls for murder investigation

articles.summary:
Activists and lawyers in Ecuador are calling for a murder investigation after Polish anti-corruption campaigner Monika Silva Koniuszek was found dead in her home in Montañita on June 8.

Ecuador’s interior minister said the initial hypothesis was suicide, but a postmortem released Friday found that the 41-year-old died from a blow to the head and strangulation, according to the Guardian.

Silva Koniuszek had spent years reporting alleged environmental crimes, land trafficking and corruption in E
articles.content:
Activists and lawyers in Ecuador are calling for a murder investigation after Polish anti-corruption campaigner Monika Silva Koniuszek was found dead in her home in Montañita on June 8.

Ecuador’s interior minister said the initial hypothesis was suicide, but a postmortem released Friday found that the 41-year-old died from a blow to the head and strangulation, according to the Guardian.

Silva Koniuszek had spent years reporting alleged environmental crimes, land trafficking and corruption in Ecuador. Friends and colleagues said she had recently been investigating allegations involving Noboa Trading, a company linked to the family of President Daniel Noboa, as well as politically connected figures in Santa Elena province. The article said those allegations had not been resolved in court.

Friends said Silva Koniuszek had reported receiving death threats and had told them she was being followed. Polish authorities have requested legal cooperation from Ecuador and said they want to be closely involved in the investigation.

Poland’s embassy said it hoped Ecuadorian authorities would carry out a swift, independent and transparent inquiry and stressed the importance of protecting human rights defenders, journalists and civic activists.

articles.headline: Iran’s president to visit Pakistan after US-Iran talks in Switzerland
articles.deck: Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian is scheduled to visit Pakistan on Tuesday, one day after the first round of US-Iran talks in Burgenstock, Switzerland, produced a 60-day road...
articles.summary:
Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian is scheduled to visit Pakistan on Tuesday, one day after the first round of US-Iran talks in Burgenstock, Switzerland, produced a 60-day roadmap toward a possible final agreement.

During the visit, Pezeshkian is expected to meet Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, President Asif Ali Zardari and other senior Pakistani officials. Pakistan’s Foreign Ministry said the two sides will discuss the Switzerland talks as well as trade, energy, border security and regional c
articles.content:
Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian is scheduled to visit Pakistan on Tuesday, one day after the first round of US-Iran talks in Burgenstock, Switzerland, produced a 60-day roadmap toward a possible final agreement.

During the visit, Pezeshkian is expected to meet Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, President Asif Ali Zardari and other senior Pakistani officials. Pakistan’s Foreign Ministry said the two sides will discuss the Switzerland talks as well as trade, energy, border security and regional connectivity.

Pakistan helped mediate the recent US-Iran discussions, according to officials from both countries. Analysts cited by Al Jazeera said the visit signals Iran’s interest in strengthening Pakistan’s role as a regional interlocutor while also showing domestic audiences that Tehran is pursuing diplomacy through regional partners.

Relations between Iran and Pakistan had been strained after cross-border missile strikes in January 2024, but both countries moved to restore ties. Since then, the two governments have increased high-level contacts and signed a series of bilateral agreements.

Former Pakistani ambassador Asif Durrani told Al Jazeera that sanctions relief remains a major issue for Iran and said technical talks are continuing after the Switzerland meeting.

articles.summary:
European Union officials are set to meet Taliban representatives in Brussels to discuss irregular migration from Afghanistan and the return of Afghans whose asylum applications have been rejected, after Belgium issued five one-day visas for the delegation.

The meeting would be the first time the EU has hosted Taliban officials since the group returned to power in Afghanistan in August 2021. The European Commission said the talks do not amount to formal recognition of the Taliban government.

Co
articles.content:
European Union officials are set to meet Taliban representatives in Brussels to discuss irregular migration from Afghanistan and the return of Afghans whose asylum applications have been rejected, after Belgium issued five one-day visas for the delegation.

The meeting would be the first time the EU has hosted Taliban officials since the group returned to power in Afghanistan in August 2021. The European Commission said the talks do not amount to formal recognition of the Taliban government.

Commission spokesman Markus Lammert said member states are examining ways to return people who have committed serious crimes or pose security threats. A letter reported by Reuters said the meeting would focus on the return and readmission of Afghan nationals without a right to stay in the EU.

Rights groups, including Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International, said any engagement with the Taliban should prioritize human rights and warned against deportations to Afghanistan. The country remains in a humanitarian crisis, with the UN World Food Programme estimating that more than 17 million Afghans face food insecurity.

EU countries received about one million asylum applications from Afghans between 2013 and 2024, according to the bloc’s migration agency. Although Afghans have had relatively high recognition rates, some member states have pressed for more returns, especially in cases involving criminal convictions.

articles.headline: Primary elections in Maryland, Utah and New York draw attention ahead of US midterms
articles.deck: Voters in Maryland, Utah and New York are holding primary elections Tuesday, while South Carolina is conducting runoff races, in contests that will help shape the United States ...
articles.summary:
Voters in Maryland, Utah and New York are holding primary elections Tuesday, while South Carolina is conducting runoff races, in contests that will help shape the United States midterm elections later this year.

In Utah, one closely watched race is the Democratic primary for the state’s 1st Congressional District after a court-ordered map change kept Salt Lake County more intact, creating a district viewed as more favorable to Democrats. Former Representative Ben McAdams, state Senator Nate Blo
articles.content:
Voters in Maryland, Utah and New York are holding primary elections Tuesday, while South Carolina is conducting runoff races, in contests that will help shape the United States midterm elections later this year.

In Utah, one closely watched race is the Democratic primary for the state’s 1st Congressional District after a court-ordered map change kept Salt Lake County more intact, creating a district viewed as more favorable to Democrats. Former Representative Ben McAdams, state Senator Nate Blouin and two other candidates are competing for the nomination. The Republican nominee, Riley Owen, has already secured his party’s spot in the November election.

In Maryland, attention is focused on the Democratic primary for state Senate President Bill Ferguson’s seat. Ferguson is facing a challenge from Bobby LaPin after criticism from some Democrats over Ferguson’s opposition last year to a proposal to redraw Maryland’s congressional map.

South Carolina’s Republican runoff for governor pits Attorney General Alan Wilson against Lieutenant Governor Pamela Evette after neither won a majority in the June 9 primary. President Donald Trump initially endorsed Evette and later endorsed both candidates ahead of the runoff.

In New York, Democrats are competing to replace Representative Jerry Nadler in the 12th Congressional District after his planned retirement. Candidates include George Conway, Jack Schlossberg, Alex Bores and Micah Lasher in a crowded field centered in Manhattan.

articles.headline: EU plans Brussels meeting with Taliban delegation on Afghan returns
articles.deck: A Taliban delegation is expected in Brussels for talks with European Union officials on the return of Afghan nationals whose asylum applications have been rejected, after Belgiu...
articles.summary:
A Taliban delegation is expected in Brussels for talks with European Union officials on the return of Afghan nationals whose asylum applications have been rejected, after Belgium issued one-day visas for the visit.

The European Commission invited the delegation as part of discussions on irregular migration and deportations, although the EU does not formally recognize the Taliban administration. Belgium said the five visas were granted Monday after a security assessment and are valid only in Bel
articles.content:
A Taliban delegation is expected in Brussels for talks with European Union officials on the return of Afghan nationals whose asylum applications have been rejected, after Belgium issued one-day visas for the visit.

The European Commission invited the delegation as part of discussions on irregular migration and deportations, although the EU does not formally recognize the Taliban administration. Belgium said the five visas were granted Monday after a security assessment and are valid only in Belgium for one day.

EU officials and Belgium said hosting the meeting does not amount to recognition of the Taliban government. Rights groups, including Human Rights Watch, criticized the plan and said cooperation on returns undermines the bloc’s stated human rights commitments.

About 20 of the EU’s 27 member states have expressed interest in returning some Afghans without a legal right to remain, particularly those convicted of serious crimes, according to EU officials. Commission spokesman Markus Lammert said the focus is on people who pose security threats.

The talks come as Afghanistan faces a humanitarian crisis. United Nations agencies have reported widespread hunger and economic hardship since the Taliban returned to power in 2021 and imposed broad restrictions on women and girls.

articles.headline: France issues highest heat alert as temperatures climb
articles.deck: France placed 54 departments under its highest heat-wave alert on Tuesday as extreme temperatures continued across most of the country.
articles.summary:
France placed 54 departments under its highest heat-wave alert on Tuesday as extreme temperatures continued across most of the country. Meteo France said the heat is expected to last at least through the end of the week, with daytime highs above 40 degrees Celsius in many areas and the possibility of record temperatures for this time of year.

The alert has disrupted schools, rail service and sports events in a country where air-conditioning is not widespread. Authorities have also reported abou
articles.content:
France placed 54 departments under its highest heat-wave alert on Tuesday as extreme temperatures continued across most of the country. Meteo France said the heat is expected to last at least through the end of the week, with daytime highs above 40 degrees Celsius in many areas and the possibility of record temperatures for this time of year.

The alert has disrupted schools, rail service and sports events in a country where air-conditioning is not widespread. Authorities have also reported about 20 drowning deaths since the weekend.

Meteo France said temperatures are remaining high overnight as well as during the day. The agency compared the current conditions to the August 2003 heat wave, which was linked to an estimated 15,000 deaths in France.

European Union climate data shows Europe has warmed faster than the global average since the 1980s. The World Health Organization’s Europe office said this month that more than 200,000 people in Europe died from heat-related causes over the past four years, and that many of those deaths were preventable.

The EU’s Copernicus Climate Change Service said 2024 was the hottest year on record globally and in Europe, and that the continent recorded its second-highest number of heat-stress days.

articles.headline: Chinese Carrier Liaoning Returns to Qingdao After 40-Day Western Pacific Deployment
articles.deck: China’s Liaoning carrier strike group returned to Qingdao on June 22 after a 40-day deployment in the South China Sea and Philippine Sea, according to the People’s Liberation Ar...
articles.summary:
China’s Liaoning carrier strike group returned to Qingdao on June 22 after a 40-day deployment in the South China Sea and Philippine Sea, according to the People’s Liberation Army Navy and reporting by USNI News.

Chinese military statements said the deployment involved open-sea combat training in the South China Sea and western Pacific. Japanese officials previously tracked the group in the Philippine Sea, where it operated with escorts including the cruiser Wuxi, destroyer Kaifeng, frigate Luo
articles.content:
China’s Liaoning carrier strike group returned to Qingdao on June 22 after a 40-day deployment in the South China Sea and Philippine Sea, according to the People’s Liberation Army Navy and reporting by USNI News.

Chinese military statements said the deployment involved open-sea combat training in the South China Sea and western Pacific. Japanese officials previously tracked the group in the Philippine Sea, where it operated with escorts including the cruiser Wuxi, destroyer Kaifeng, frigate Luohe, and fast combat support ship Hulunhu.

Chinese state media released video showing day and night flight operations by carrier-based J-15 aircraft, including midair refueling with a People’s Liberation Army Air Force YU-20 tanker. State media also said Liaoning’s group conducted joint exercises with the amphibious assault ship Anhui and another surface action group in the western Pacific.

Japan’s Maritime Self-Defense Force monitored the deployment, as it routinely does when Chinese naval ships operate near Japan or move through nearby straits. Chinese state media accused Japanese ships and aircraft of close-range tracking and said the Chinese force maintained a high state of alert throughout the mission.

Japan’s Joint Staff Office reported sightings of elements of the strike group during transits near Miyako Island and Okinawa and said carrier aircraft conducted takeoffs and landings in the Philippine Sea from May 26 to 28.

The deployment ended with the carrier group returning to its home port in Qingdao.

articles.headline: Baykar and Leonardo Conduct Autonomy Trial Between Kizilelma Drone and M-346 Jet
articles.deck: Turkey’s Baykar and Italy’s Leonardo said they completed the first live trials of their K-SWARM program, testing coordinated operations between a Kizilelma uncrewed combat aircr...
articles.summary:
Turkey’s Baykar and Italy’s Leonardo said they completed the first live trials of their K-SWARM program, testing coordinated operations between a Kizilelma uncrewed combat aircraft and an M-346 fighter-trainer.

The companies said the trials took place last month at Baykar’s test center in Çorlu, Turkey, using a Leonardo-owned M-346 Fighter Attack aircraft and a Kizilelma drone. During the test, the drone taxied and took off autonomously, joined the M-346 in formation, and then responded to comm
articles.content:
Turkey’s Baykar and Italy’s Leonardo said they completed the first live trials of their K-SWARM program, testing coordinated operations between a Kizilelma uncrewed combat aircraft and an M-346 fighter-trainer.

The companies said the trials took place last month at Baykar’s test center in Çorlu, Turkey, using a Leonardo-owned M-346 Fighter Attack aircraft and a Kizilelma drone. During the test, the drone taxied and took off autonomously, joined the M-346 in formation, and then responded to commands from the two-person crew in the jet to perform maneuvers including position changes, separations, and rejoins.

According to the companies, the drone carried out those actions autonomously after receiving initial commands through a newly integrated avionics and data exchange system. An Italian Air Force T-346A served as a chase aircraft during the trials.

The test is part of a broader effort to develop crewed-uncrewed teaming, in which piloted aircraft direct drones during combat missions such as reconnaissance, jamming, decoy operations, or weapons employment. Baykar and Leonardo said earlier simulator work in Italy helped prepare for the live phase of testing.

The Kizilelma is one of a limited number of fighter-type drone projects to progress to flight hardware. Baykar has presented it as a future companion to Turkey’s TF Kaan fighter. The companies said additional K-SWARM trials in the coming months will add complexity and more advanced cooperation between platforms.

The program’s longer-term goal is to increase the drone’s autonomy while keeping human pilots in control of mission decisions.

articles.headline: U.S. Strike on Suspected Drug Boat in Caribbean Kills Two, Rescue Effort Follows
articles.summary:
A U.S. strike on a suspected drug-trafficking boat in the Caribbean on June 21 killed two men, according to U.S. Southern Command and Coast Guard statements cited by USNI News.

Six other people survived the initial strike, and the U.S. Coast Guard began search-and-rescue operations before Venezuela’s rescue coordination center took over, according to a Coast Guard Atlantic Area post on X.

U.S. Southern Command did not specify the location of the strike, but USNI News reported it likely occurre
articles.content:
A U.S. strike on a suspected drug-trafficking boat in the Caribbean on June 21 killed two men, according to U.S. Southern Command and Coast Guard statements cited by USNI News.

Six other people survived the initial strike, and the U.S. Coast Guard began search-and-rescue operations before Venezuela’s rescue coordination center took over, according to a Coast Guard Atlantic Area post on X.

U.S. Southern Command did not specify the location of the strike, but USNI News reported it likely occurred off Venezuela because Venezuelan authorities assumed responsibility for the rescue effort.

USNI News said the strike was the first in the Caribbean since early May and the 66th U.S. strike on suspected drug boats since September 2025. The outlet also reported that the confirmed death toll from such strikes has reached 210, while noting the number could be higher because some survivors from earlier strikes are presumed dead.

The report said the Trump administration has described the targeted vessels as carrying fentanyl and cocaine, but added that the effect of the strikes on U.S. overdose deaths remains unclear. It cited prior reporting that most fentanyl entering the United States comes through Mexico and that precursor supplies often come from China.

The helicopter was operating from Coast Guard Air Station Sitka when it went down at about 10:07 a.m. local time, according to the Coast Guard. Sitka Fire and Rescue crews reached the scene at about 11 a.m. and transported the crew members to Mt. Edgecumbe Medical Center.

The Coast Guard said no de
articles.content:
A U.S. Coast Guard MH-60 Jayhawk helicopter crashed during a training flight near Harbor Mountain in Sitka, Alaska, on June 22, and all four people aboard were taken to a hospital, the service said.

The helicopter was operating from Coast Guard Air Station Sitka when it went down at about 10:07 a.m. local time, according to the Coast Guard. Sitka Fire and Rescue crews reached the scene at about 11 a.m. and transported the crew members to Mt. Edgecumbe Medical Center.

The Coast Guard said no deaths were reported.

In a separate statement, the service said the cause of the crash was not yet known and that a formal investigation was underway.

The Coast Guard said the immediate priority was the safety, well-being, and recovery of the crew members.

articles.headline: L3Harris Details Rapid Conversion of Interim Air Force One Aircraft
articles.deck: L3Harris says it converted a Boeing 747-8i obtained by the U.S.
articles.summary:
L3Harris says it converted a Boeing 747-8i obtained by the U.S. government from Qatar into an interim presidential aircraft in about 10 months, providing a temporary replacement as the long-delayed VC-25B program continues.

In an interview with The War Zone, Jason Lambert, president of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance at L3Harris, said the company worked with the Air Force around the clock to deliver what is now being called the VC-25B bridge aircraft. He said the project also incl
articles.content:
L3Harris says it converted a Boeing 747-8i obtained by the U.S. government from Qatar into an interim presidential aircraft in about 10 months, providing a temporary replacement as the long-delayed VC-25B program continues.

In an interview with The War Zone, Jason Lambert, president of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance at L3Harris, said the company worked with the Air Force around the clock to deliver what is now being called the VC-25B bridge aircraft. He said the project also included training and sustainment work for the Presidential Airlift Group and broader support for the future VC-25B fleet.

Lambert said the Air Force pursued an interim aircraft because the two current VC-25A aircraft are about 35 years old and the Boeing-led replacement program has been delayed for years. He described L3Harris as well suited for the work because it specializes in modifying existing aircraft for specialized missions and already provides the secure senior leader communications systems used on presidential aircraft.

According to Lambert, much of the aircraft’s interior structure was retained to reduce schedule risk, while communications, security screening, and certain finish details were updated. He said U.S. government and company specialists conducted extensive inspections and security work before modifications began to ensure the aircraft was safe for presidential use.

Lambert declined to discuss classified details about survivability, electromagnetic hardening, command-and-control capabilities, and how the interim aircraft compares with the full VC-25B configuration. He said the aircraft is intended for overseas use and that White House and Air Force officials were involved in design decisions, including exterior paint and interior presentation.

The company said the program showed that a compressed schedule is possible when government and industry align on requirements and maintain tight control over design changes.

articles.headline: ISW Says New Lebanon Ceasefire Mechanism Could Limit Israeli Action Against Hezbollah
articles.deck: A new mechanism created to oversee the Lebanon ceasefire could restrict Israel’s ability to act against Hezbollah violations, according to a June 22 special report by the Instit...
articles.summary:
A new mechanism created to oversee the Lebanon ceasefire could restrict Israel’s ability to act against Hezbollah violations, according to a June 22 special report by the Institute for the Study of War and the Critical Threats Project.

The report said U.S., Iranian, Qatari, Pakistani, and Lebanese participants agreed to establish a “deconfliction cell” following June 21 talks in Switzerland. ISW said the arrangement appears to replace the post-2024 ceasefire monitoring system that had included
articles.content:
A new mechanism created to oversee the Lebanon ceasefire could restrict Israel’s ability to act against Hezbollah violations, according to a June 22 special report by the Institute for the Study of War and the Critical Threats Project.

The report said U.S., Iranian, Qatari, Pakistani, and Lebanese participants agreed to establish a “deconfliction cell” following June 21 talks in Switzerland. ISW said the arrangement appears to replace the post-2024 ceasefire monitoring system that had included Israel, Lebanon, the United States, France, and the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon.

According to the report, the earlier mechanism allowed Israel to raise alleged Hezbollah violations and, when Lebanese authorities did not address them, to conduct military action. ISW said the new structure excludes Israel while including Iran, which it said could make it harder for Israel to respond directly through the new forum.

The report also said Iran secured economic measures through a U.S. Treasury sanctions waiver covering certain Iranian oil and petrochemical exports through Aug. 21 and through Iranian media reports of an understanding with Qatar to help unfreeze Iranian assets. Qatar had not publicly confirmed that separate understanding at the time of the report.

ISW said Iranian officials did not signal new nuclear concessions after the Switzerland talks. An Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson said implementation of mutual obligations would depend on other parts of the memorandum, including ceasefires and economic relief. U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran would need to accept International Atomic Energy Agency inspections, while the Iranian spokesperson said Tehran had made no new commitments beyond existing legal frameworks.

The report also noted that U.S. and Iranian delegations agreed to establish a communication line to avoid military incidents in the Strait of Hormuz as commercial shipping continues through the waterway.

articles.headline: U.S. Aircraft Carriers Remain Deployed in Middle East as Fleet Operations Continue Worldwide
articles.summary:
Two U.S. aircraft carriers remained in the Middle East region as of June 22, while other carriers conducted training, maintenance, or transit operations elsewhere, according to The War Zone’s weekly review of open-source fleet movements.

The report said the United States had lifted its naval blockade of Iranian ports following implementation of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, but that major naval assets remained in the area during ongoing negotiations. U.S. Central Command said force
articles.content:
Two U.S. aircraft carriers remained in the Middle East region as of June 22, while other carriers conducted training, maintenance, or transit operations elsewhere, according to The War Zone’s weekly review of open-source fleet movements.

The report said the United States had lifted its naval blockade of Iranian ports following implementation of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, but that major naval assets remained in the area during ongoing negotiations. U.S. Central Command said forces would stay in place for now to support freedom of navigation and monitor compliance with the agreement.

USS Abraham Lincoln was reported conducting flight operations in the Central Command area of responsibility as of June 21, and USS George H.W. Bush was last observed in the region on June 20. The future size and duration of the U.S. naval presence there remains unclear.

Elsewhere, USS Nimitz arrived at Naval Station Mayport in Florida on June 16 after taking part in Southern Seas 2026 and is continuing its homeport shift to Norfolk. USS Dwight D. Eisenhower left Norfolk on June 17 for carrier qualification operations and is preparing for a deployment expected in early 2027, according to the report.

USS Theodore Roosevelt was operating off the U.S. West Coast in training exercises intended to improve strike group readiness, while USS Carl Vinson was moored in San Diego. In the western Pacific, USS George Washington took part in Valiant Shield 2026 after a port visit to Guam, operating alongside U.S. and Japanese ships and aircraft.

The report said carrier positions are approximate and based on publicly available information.

articles.headline: Ukrainian Strikes Near Moscow and Voronezh Highlight Expanding Long-Range Campaign, ISW Says
articles.deck: Ukrainian forces struck targets near Moscow and in Voronezh Oblast on June 21 and 22, according to the Institute for the Study of War, as Kyiv continued to expand the range and ...
articles.summary:
Ukrainian forces struck targets near Moscow and in Voronezh Oblast on June 21 and 22, according to the Institute for the Study of War, as Kyiv continued to expand the range and intensity of its long-range strike campaign against Russian military and industrial infrastructure.

ISW said the Ukrainian General Staff reported a strike on the Dubna Space Communications Center in Moscow Oblast, a satellite communications hub northwest of Moscow. Russian officials said air defenses intercepted many dro
articles.content:
Ukrainian forces struck targets near Moscow and in Voronezh Oblast on June 21 and 22, according to the Institute for the Study of War, as Kyiv continued to expand the range and intensity of its long-range strike campaign against Russian military and industrial infrastructure.

ISW said the Ukrainian General Staff reported a strike on the Dubna Space Communications Center in Moscow Oblast, a satellite communications hub northwest of Moscow. Russian officials said air defenses intercepted many drones approaching the capital and reported no major disruption to communications. The think tank also cited reports that Russian authorities have been strengthening air defenses around Moscow, including possible redeployments from frontline areas.

The assessment said Ukrainian forces also struck an enterprise in Voronezh City that the Ukrainian General Staff said produces components for Kh-101 and Iskander-K cruise missiles and Pantsir-S1 air defense systems. Russian regional officials acknowledged damage at a production facility, while Russian sources offered differing accounts of the weapon used. ISW said it could not independently confirm the specific munition.

According to the report, Ukrainian strikes have increasingly targeted Russian air defenses, oil infrastructure, vehicles, bridges, ports, and logistics routes in both Russia and occupied Ukrainian territory. ISW said this campaign is creating additional pressure on Russia’s air defense network and contributing to fuel shortages and transport disruptions in occupied Crimea and other areas.

The report also described continued fighting across multiple sectors of the front, including reported Russian advances north of Kupyansk, ongoing battles near Kostyantynivka and Pokrovsk, and Ukrainian strikes on Russian command posts, bridges, rail infrastructure, and drone training sites. Russian forces also launched missile and drone attacks on Ukrainian civilian and energy infrastructure overnight, according to Ukrainian officials cited in the assessment.

ISW said the broader effect of the Ukrainian strike campaign is to force Russia to spread limited defensive assets across a wide area while complicating logistics and industrial support for its military operations.

articles.headline: Germany Military Strategy Draws Debate Over Readiness and Timelines
articles.deck: Germany’s new military strategy has prompted debate over whether it provides a clear path to strengthen the Bundeswehr and meet NATO responsibilities.
articles.summary:
Germany’s new military strategy has prompted debate over whether it provides a clear path to strengthen the Bundeswehr and meet NATO responsibilities. The document, released in late April, identifies Russia as the central threat, calls for stronger European conventional defense within NATO, and highlights the growing role of drones, artificial intelligence, long-range precision weapons, and multi-domain operations.

Roderich Kiesewetter, a member of the German parliament from the Christian Democ
articles.content:
Germany’s new military strategy has prompted debate over whether it provides a clear path to strengthen the Bundeswehr and meet NATO responsibilities. The document, released in late April, identifies Russia as the central threat, calls for stronger European conventional defense within NATO, and highlights the growing role of drones, artificial intelligence, long-range precision weapons, and multi-domain operations.

Roderich Kiesewetter, a member of the German parliament from the Christian Democratic Union and a retired Bundeswehr colonel, argues that the strategy is more analytical than operational. He writes that it does not set concrete force-building targets, annual benchmarks, or structural reforms that would allow Germany to accelerate military readiness.

The strategy sets 2029 as the point by which the Bundeswehr should be able to defend Germany, while some major capabilities, including digitized connectivity, multidimensional situational awareness, expanded air defense, and industrial and logistical resilience, are not expected until 2035. The goal of developing Europe’s strongest conventional army is projected for 2039. Kiesewetter argues that these timelines do not match the urgency of the threat assessments cited in the document.

He also says Germany remains short of current NATO personnel and equipment commitments and contends that planned force levels may be insufficient if the United States reduces conventional forces in Europe. He points to procurement delays, a lack of structural reform in the defense ministry and armed forces, and the absence of broader debate on measures such as conscription or mandatory national service.

Kiesewetter writes that the strategy appears aimed in part at reassuring allies ahead of a NATO summit by presenting Germany as ready to assume a larger leadership role in European defense. He argues that the value of the strategy will depend on whether policymakers translate its analysis into concrete reforms, faster capability development, and clearer decisions on burden-sharing, force structure, and deterrence.

articles.headline: Newfoundland and Labrador health agency apologizes for phishing test sent to staff
articles.deck: Newfoundland and Labrador Health Services apologized after an internal phishing awareness test offered employees and physicians what appeared to be an additional paid vacation day.
articles.summary:
Newfoundland and Labrador Health Services apologized after an internal phishing awareness test offered employees and physicians what appeared to be an additional paid vacation day. The organization said the exercise was inappropriate and said it is reviewing how future cybersecurity tests are designed and communicated.

The email referenced staff work on the CorCare software rollout and invited recipients to click a button to redeem the extra day off. Instead, employees who clicked were marked a
articles.content:
Newfoundland and Labrador Health Services apologized after an internal phishing awareness test offered employees and physicians what appeared to be an additional paid vacation day. The organization said the exercise was inappropriate and said it is reviewing how future cybersecurity tests are designed and communicated.

The email referenced staff work on the CorCare software rollout and invited recipients to click a button to redeem the extra day off. Instead, employees who clicked were marked as having failed the test. Interim chief executive Ron Johnson said the scenario did not reflect the organization's values and that officials would review how it was approved.

The Registered Nurses Union said members viewed the exercise as insensitive because healthcare workers are already dealing with staffing shortages, burnout and difficulty securing time off. Union president Yvette Coffey said cybersecurity education is important but should be carried out with greater judgment and respect. articles.headline: Alan Greenspan dies at 100
articles.deck: Alan Greenspan, who served as chair of the Federal Reserve from 1987 to 2006, died June 22 at his home in Washington at age 100, according to statements cited by Bloomberg and T...
articles.summary:
Alan Greenspan, who served as chair of the Federal Reserve from 1987 to 2006, died June 22 at his home in Washington at age 100, according to statements cited by Bloomberg and The Washington Post. His wife, NBC News correspondent Andrea Mitchell, said the cause was complications of Parkinson's disease.

Greenspan led the central bank through the 1987 stock market crash, the 1991 recession, the late-1990s expansion, the dot-com downturn and the period after the Sept. 11, 2001 attacks. He was appo
articles.content:
Alan Greenspan, who served as chair of the Federal Reserve from 1987 to 2006, died June 22 at his home in Washington at age 100, according to statements cited by Bloomberg and The Washington Post. His wife, NBC News correspondent Andrea Mitchell, said the cause was complications of Parkinson's disease.

Greenspan led the central bank through the 1987 stock market crash, the 1991 recession, the late-1990s expansion, the dot-com downturn and the period after the Sept. 11, 2001 attacks. He was appointed to five terms by four presidents and became one of the most prominent central bankers in the United States.

His record was later reassessed after the 2007-2009 financial crisis. Critics said the Fed under Greenspan kept interest rates too low for too long and did not use its regulatory authority aggressively enough as mortgage lending and derivatives markets expanded. Greenspan acknowledged mistakes in congressional testimony in 2008, saying he had assumed financial institutions would protect their own interests.

articles.headline: [Microsoft outlines Windows 11 26H2 rollout and support timeline]
articles.deck: Microsoft said Windows 11 version 26H2 is expected to reach general availability in the coming months and will share the same core platform as versions 24H2 and 25H2.
articles.summary:
Microsoft said Windows 11 version 26H2 is expected to reach general availability in the coming months and will share the same core platform as versions 24H2 and 25H2. Because of that, the company plans to deliver it through an enablement package that activates new features and updates the version number.

Microsoft said devices running Windows 11 26H1 will not be offered 26H2 through that path. The company described 26H1 as a separate release for devices using Qualcomm Snapdragon X2 Series proce
articles.content:
Microsoft said Windows 11 version 26H2 is expected to reach general availability in the coming months and will share the same core platform as versions 24H2 and 25H2. Because of that, the company plans to deliver it through an enablement package that activates new features and updates the version number.

Microsoft said devices running Windows 11 26H1 will not be offered 26H2 through that path. The company described 26H1 as a separate release for devices using Qualcomm Snapdragon X2 Series processors and other new hardware platforms.

Preview builds of 26H2 are available to Windows Insiders in the Experimental channel, and Microsoft said there are also upgrade paths from the Dev and Beta channels.

The company is also testing changes to Windows Search intended to improve results for app names when users enter misspellings, missing letters, extra letters, or partial words. Microsoft said it has also adjusted ranking to surface more relevant settings results. March Rogers, partner director of product design for Windows, said local files are prioritized in the updated search experience and that web suggestions can be turned off in Settings.

Microsoft said support for Windows 11 24H2 will end on October 13, 2026, for Home and Pro users, while Enterprise and Education editions will receive support until October 13, 2027. The company said 26H2 will receive 24 months of support for Home and Pro editions and 36 months for Enterprise and Education.

Londo
articles.content:
London Hydro said it is investigating a security incident that may have affected personal information tied to some customer accounts. The Ontario utility, which serves more than 160,000 customers in and around London, said it has begun notifying affected customers.

The company said the information that may have been exposed includes names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, account and billing numbers, service addresses, pricing plans, contract start dates, and meter information.

London Hydro said the incident did not involve banking information, payment card details, dates of birth, government-issued identification numbers, or other sensitive financial data.

The utility has not said how many customers were affected, how the incident occurred, or whether data was accessed or removed. It also has not said whether operational or grid-related systems were involved.

London Hydro advised customers to watch for suspicious communications, unexpected bills, unfamiliar account activity, or requests to change payment arrangements. It also said it does not request banking details by email, phone, or text message.

articles.content:
Lucid Motors said it is laying off 18 percent of its workforce, or about 1,500 employees, and eliminating the second production shift at its factory in Casa Grande, Arizona. The company said the move is part of a restructuring effort under new CEO Silvio Napoli aimed at simplifying operations and aligning production with expected demand.

The company also said it eliminated the chief operating officer position, and former interim CEO Marc Winterhoff has left. Lucid said the restructuring is expected to generate about $158 million in annualized savings and cost about $32 million in severance. The changes come as the company prepares to launch its lower-cost Cosmos SUV and continues work on an autonomous vehicle partnership with Uber and Nuro.

articles.content:

articles.content:
Researchers from Huazhong University of Science and Technology and VIVO AI Lab introduced Moebius, an image inpainting model with about 0.22 billion parameters. The project says the model is intended to provide lower computational cost while maintaining performance comparable to larger inpainting systems.

The authors say Moebius uses a redesigned diffusion backbone and a distillation strategy to improve efficiency and image quality. They report that the model uses less than 2 percent of the parameters of FLUX.1-Fill-Dev and achieves more than a 15-fold reduction in total inference time while performing competitively across several natural-scene and portrait benchmarks.

articles.content: TrendForce says shortages in mainstream DRAM are pushing buyers toward older memory products such as DDR2 and DDR3, increasing prices for those components as well, according to The Register. articles.summary:
Nvidia used the ISC High Performance 2026 conference in Hamburg to outline its push to apply agentic AI to scientific computing, describing new software and hardware it says will support research workflows that combine simulation, AI models, and automated tools.

Nvidia executive Dion Harris said the company expects Los Alamos National Laboratory’s Mission and Vision systems to be the first supercomputers built around this approach when they come online. He said the systems will use Nvidia’s for
articles.content:
Nvidia used the ISC High Performance 2026 conference in Hamburg to outline its push to apply agentic AI to scientific computing, describing new software and hardware it says will support research workflows that combine simulation, AI models, and automated tools.

Nvidia executive Dion Harris said the company expects Los Alamos National Laboratory’s Mission and Vision systems to be the first supercomputers built around this approach when they come online. He said the systems will use Nvidia’s forthcoming Vera Rubin platform, along with new software packages called ALCHEMI, DAQIRI, and cuPhoton.

According to Nvidia, ALCHEMI is intended for chemistry and materials research, DAQIRI is aimed at real-time AI processing for scientific instruments, and cuPhoton is designed to process large camera and telescope data sets. Harris said cuPhoton testing with 32 Grace Blackwell superchips on Rubin Observatory data showed major gains in loading, reading, and signal-processing speed, though those figures were provided by Nvidia.

The company said Vera Rubin NVL racks, expected in the fourth quarter of 2026, will support up to 144 GPUs per rack and deliver 5 petaFLOPS of FP64 performance. Harris said the design also increases memory bandwidth compared with Blackwell and is intended for memory-intensive scientific workloads.

Asked why agentic AI is needed in scientific research, Harris said it is not required but can help scientists work at larger scale by continuously reviewing technical literature, using tools, and running workflows. The Register reported that Nvidia also said 35 new European supercomputers using its technology came online over the past year.

articles.summary:
Canada’s federal government has released a nuclear strategy that calls for building up to 10 new reactors over the next 15 years, with at least one under construction outside Ontario by 2035. The plan was announced June 22 by Energy and Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson.

According to the strategy, construction should begin on two new large-scale reactors by 2035, with five more planned or under development by 2040. It also calls for a Canadian-made microreactor to be finalized by 2035 and
articles.content:
Canada’s federal government has released a nuclear strategy that calls for building up to 10 new reactors over the next 15 years, with at least one under construction outside Ontario by 2035. The plan was announced June 22 by Energy and Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson.

According to the strategy, construction should begin on two new large-scale reactors by 2035, with five more planned or under development by 2040. It also calls for a Canadian-made microreactor to be finalized by 2035 and deployed to a remote community by the late 2030s.

Officials from Natural Resources Canada said construction of the proposed reactors could cost more than C$100 billion. The strategy does not specify how the projects would be financed, though officials pointed to the Canadian Infrastructure Bank and the Canada Growth Fund as possible sources.

Hodgson said the plan is tied to Canada’s goal of doubling electricity-grid capacity by 2050 and expanding nuclear-sector employment. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said he was not persuaded by the announcement and argued that the government needed to show results rather than plans.

The strategy also aims to expand exports of Canadian Candu reactor technology and increase uranium exports. Government officials said Prime Minister Mark Carney was screened from the strategy because Candu competes with a reactor model co-owned by Brookfield, a company in which he previously held stock options and deferred shares that were later placed in a blind trust.
