articles.summary:
A commentary article published by IPVM argues that police searches of stored license plate reader data should require a warrant, citing several criminal and disciplinary cases in which officers allegedly used Flock Safety systems to monitor people they knew personally.

The article highlights the June 18, 2026 arrest of the police chief of Holiday Hills, Illinois, who prosecutors said used Prairie Grove’s Flock system and the Illinois State Police LEADS database to track six people, including fo
articles.content:
A commentary article published by IPVM argues that police searches of stored license plate reader data should require a warrant, citing several criminal and disciplinary cases in which officers allegedly used Flock Safety systems to monitor people they knew personally.

The article highlights the June 18, 2026 arrest of the police chief of Holiday Hills, Illinois, who prosecutors said used Prairie Grove’s Flock system and the Illinois State Police LEADS database to track six people, including former romantic partners and one person’s former boyfriend. The article says the alleged misconduct spanned from February 2024 to November 2025.

IPVM also cites other reported cases involving police officials in Georgia, Idaho, Kansas, Wisconsin, and California. It argues that those cases show license plate data can function as a tool for tracking individuals, even though the system records vehicles.

The article notes that civil-liberties groups including the ACLU, EFF, and Institute for Justice have supported warrant-based use of license plate reader systems while accepting their use as investigative tools. It also cites existing legal standards requiring warrants for GPS tracking, historical cell-site data, and wiretaps, while noting that emergency exceptions can still allow warrantless access in urgent situations.

The piece is an opinion argument rather than a straight news report and centers on IPVM’s position that judicial approval should be required before routine searches of stored plate-reader data.

articles.content:
An open-source hardware project on GitHub outlines Optocam Zero, a compact digital camera built around a Raspberry Pi Zero and off-the-shelf components.

According to the project documentation, the camera is designed to be pocket-sized and relatively easy to assemble. Listed features include an autofocus camera module, eight photo filters, USB-C charging, a replaceable 14500 lithium-ion battery, a 1.4-inch display, and image transfer through a custom hotspot interface. The project also includes GIF recording and playback.

The specifications listed in the repository include 2592-by-2592 JPEG image capture, a 240-by-240 display, a preview frame rate of 15 to 20 frames per second, a 22-second boot time, and 70 to 80 minutes of use per charge. The project states that the enclosure is fully 3D printed apart from fasteners.

The repository includes print files, a bill of materials, a build guide, and software installation instructions for people who want to assemble the camera themselves.

articles.content:
A blog post shared on Hacker News examines a range of Japanese symbols that communicate meaning without written language, from family emblems and public-service insignia to transit and driver marks.

The article describes longstanding symbols such as kamon, or family crests, as well as standardized emblems used on police cars, fire vehicles, and limited express trains. It also details marks used by drivers, including the beginner mark, elderly driver mark, hearing-impaired driver mark, and disability-related symbols.

The post further discusses public transit symbols such as the Help Mark and the maternity mark, which are intended to signal that a person may need assistance even when no condition is outwardly visible.

The author argues that these symbols function as shared cues that let people adjust their behavior without verbal explanation. The piece connects that practice to the broader social expectation in Japan of inferring situational needs from context.

articles.content:
Klue, a market-intelligence software provider, said an attacker used a compromised legacy credential tied to an integration service to access OAuth tokens and then obtain customer data from connected third-party platforms, including Salesforce. Several companies, including cybersecurity vendors, have disclosed that their CRM data was affected.

Huntress said it was among what it described as hundreds of affected Klue customers. The company said the exposed information included business contacts, price quotes, and other sales-related data and messages, but not passwords, payment card data, threat data, or engineering data tied to its agent or telemetry.

The Register reported that other companies disclosing access to CRM data through the Klue-Salesforce integration include Recorded Future, Tanium, Jamf, Gong, HackerOne, Kudelski Security, Snyk, Insurity, and Sprout Social. Klue has not publicly said how many customers were affected.

Klue said the breach occurred on June 11 and was detected the following day. The company said it disconnected integrations with Salesforce, Gong, HubSpot, SharePoint, and Google Drive, and hired CrowdStrike to assist with the investigation.

Mandiant CTO Charles Carmakal urged organizations using Klue integrations to review systems and application logs for signs of compromise and rotate credentials as needed. The Register reported that a group calling itself Icarus later posted victims on its leak site and sent extortion messages to affected organizations. Huntress said there is no evidence so far linking Icarus to the previously active extortion group ShinyHunters.

articles.summary:
AI-generated and AI-enhanced apartment photos are making it harder for renters to judge listings accurately, according to interviews and examples reported by The Verge. Renters in New York said some listings appeared significantly different in person from how they looked online.

The article describes cases in which virtual furnishings, altered room details, or entirely added features changed the apparent size or condition of an apartment. One renter said a Manhattan studio she visited was small
articles.content:
AI-generated and AI-enhanced apartment photos are making it harder for renters to judge listings accurately, according to interviews and examples reported by The Verge. Renters in New York said some listings appeared significantly different in person from how they looked online.

The article describes cases in which virtual furnishings, altered room details, or entirely added features changed the apparent size or condition of an apartment. One renter said a Manhattan studio she visited was smaller than it appeared in photos and did not include a fireplace shown in the listing images.

Virtual staging has long been used in real estate, but AI tools have made the process faster and cheaper. A Florida Realtor interviewed by The Verge said traditional virtual staging can help buyers or renters imagine renovations or new furniture, but she distinguished that from creating misleading listing images.

State rules on disclosure vary. The Verge reported that New York recently adopted a law requiring disclosure of AI in some advertising, though it is focused mainly on synthetic performers rather than real estate images. The New York secretary of state has also warned brokers against misleading AI-generated or AI-enhanced listings. California’s Altered Image Law requires disclosure when property images have been altered or enhanced with AI.

Renters interviewed by The Verge said AI use now requires closer scrutiny of photos and descriptions. Some also said listing text increasingly appears formulaic or machine-generated, adding to concerns about the accuracy of online apartment marketing.

The companies said the system would let trusted websites issue anonymous tokens that people or approved software agents could present elsewhere online.

According to Cloudflare, the goal is to reduce repeated identity checks such as CAPTCHAs while preserving privacy. The pr
articles.content:
Cloudflare said it is working with the makers of Chrome, Edge, and Firefox on a proposed protocol called Private Access Control Tokens, or PACTs, aimed at helping websites distinguish legitimate traffic from abusive requests. The companies said the system would let trusted websites issue anonymous tokens that people or approved software agents could present elsewhere online.

According to Cloudflare, the goal is to reduce repeated identity checks such as CAPTCHAs while preserving privacy. The protocol is intended to apply not only to human users but also to automated agents acting on behalf of users for authorized purposes.

Technical details are still being developed, and the proposal leaves open questions about how websites would establish what Cloudflare described as strong knowledge of personhood. The Register noted that the tokens would not by themselves address other forms of browser tracking and could introduce risks if implemented poorly.

Cloudflare CTO Dane Knecht said the system is meant to account for growing volumes of AI-related traffic on the web. Mozilla CTO Bobby Holley said the rise in automated traffic has pushed websites toward broader defenses such as paywalls, identity checks, CAPTCHAs, and tracking.

Cloudflare said the project is intended to help businesses identify genuine visitors and focus resources on preferred traffic. Website operators have increasingly raised concerns about unwanted crawler traffic, while critics say any widely adopted token system could also become a new gatekeeping mechanism for web access.

articles.content:
Nvidia just announced a warm-water cooling system that it says can dramatically reduce the amount of water a data center uses — eliminating “pretty much all water usage” inside the data center, according to an Nvidia executive in a press release.  

“The water consumption challenge for data centers is largely solved,” Josh Parker, chief sustainability officer at Nvidia, recently told Axios.

But that’s only part of the water story. As long as AI data centers run on fossil fuels — a choice tech companies are increasingly making — the savings stop at the data center’s walls.

The core issue is how Nvidia measures data center water use. According to its blog post, the company essentially draws a line around the data center. Anything inside gets counted, and anything outside gets ignored.

To be fair, Nvidia’s system does appear to deliver on its facility-level promise — the coolant runs in a closed loop, filled once and recirculated for the life of the facility, meaning no new water is consumed to cool the chips. In favorable climates, the company says, that can amount to a 100% reduction in on-site water use.

articles.content:
What does an AI company do after one of those not-acqui-hire deals, where a rival pays investors a hefty IP “licensing” fee while poaching its critical talent? For AI chipmaker Groq, the answer appears to be raise more money from investors — who were said to have profited handsomely after a deal with Nvidia in December — hire more talent, and pivot.

On Monday, Groq announced a new $650 million funding round, confirming earlier reports. The round was led by Disruptive, a Dallas-based late-stage investment firm founded by Alex Davis — who also serves as Groq’s chairman — and Infinitum, a Fort Lauderdale hedge fund.

The raise comes roughly six months after Nvidia signed a non-exclusive licensing agreement for Groq’s technology and hired away founder and CEO Jonathan Ross, president Sunny Madra, and other employees. Groq did not disclose its new valuation. It was last valued at $6.9 billion following a $750 million round in September.

articles.content:
On Friday, Claude Code creator Boris Cherny made an appearance at Meta’s @Scale conference and, surprisingly, the first question from the audience was about loops.

“Are loops the next hype cycle,” the questioner asked, “or are they for real?”

Cherny’s answer was an emphatic, “Yes, they’re for real.”

“Two years ago, we wrote source code by hand. We started to transition so agents write the code. And now we’re transitioning to the point where agents are prompting agents that then write the code,” he continued. “As big as the step from source code to agents was, loops are just as important and as big a step.”

Later in the talk (around the 32:00 mark in the YouTube video posted above), Cherny got specific about the loops he keeps running in his own work. One agent is continually looking for ways to improve the code architecture, while another looks for duplicated abstractions that can be unified. The package uses mpv and yt-dlp for playback and downloading functions, is available on GitHub, and has so far been tested on macOS.

articles.headline: NASA inspector general says Kennedy Space Center infrastructure faces strain from heavier launch demand
articles.deck: A report from NASA's Office of Inspector General said aging infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility is under growing pressure from increased launch ac...
articles.summary: A report from NASA's Office of Inspector General said aging infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility is under growing pressure from increased launch activity, especially in Florida. The report said shared systems including roads, bridges, gas lines, and a decades-old electrical distribution network may not be able to meet future demand from large vehicles such as SpaceX's Starship and Blue Origin's New Glenn. The report cited limits in the supply of gaseous nitrogen use
articles.content: A report from NASA's Office of Inspector General said aging infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility is under growing pressure from increased launch activity, especially in Florida. The report said shared systems including roads, bridges, gas lines, and a decades-old electrical distribution network may not be able to meet future demand from large vehicles such as SpaceX's Starship and Blue Origin's New Glenn. The report cited limits in the supply of gaseous nitrogen used for rocket fueling, testing, and launch operations, and said a proposed $25 million supplemental nitrogen system is not funded. It also said launch and test activity could approach or exceed the number of days in a year by late 2028 or 2029. SpaceX has told NASA it plans Starship launches from Kennedy every eight days, while SpaceX and Blue Origin each project 120 annual launches of their super heavy-lift rockets from Florida by 2035. The inspector general said NASA's inflation-adjusted budgets for construction and maintenance of launch infrastructure have declined since 2021.

articles.content: General Motors has installed about 50 FANUC robot arms at its Factory Zero electric-vehicle plant in Detroit, according to reporting cited by Ars Technica. The robots are intended to help attach vehicle components on the assembly line. The installation comes after temporary layoffs announced in March and permanent layoffs affecting another 1,200 workers at the plant in October 2025. James Cotton, president of UAW Local 22, told The Detroit News that more than 1,000 union members remain laid off indefinitely and said some of those workers could be brought back instead of adding the robots. GM's move reflects a broader industry trend toward automation, with other automakers including Stellantis, Ford, and Hyundai also expanding robot use in manufacturing. Union officials and workers said automation could affect employment and wages, while company and startup leaders have presented robotics as a way to expand manufacturing capacity.

articles.content: A shareholder lawsuit led by a Detroit pension fund was filed Monday in federal court in San Francisco accusing Uber's board and management of failing to address compliance and safety problems. The complaint alleges the company ignored repeated warnings and exposed Uber and its shareholders to legal and financial risk, including lawsuits involving allegations of sexual assault, harassment, disability access issues, and consumer complaints tied to Uber One. The suit names CEO Dara Khosrowshahi and other leaders and seeks to have them compensate the company for alleged harm, return certain compensation, and adopt stronger oversight measures. Uber disputed the claims, with a spokesperson saying the case relies on what the company described as misleading and false narratives from other lawsuits it has already contested publicly and in court.

articles.content: A Tesla Model 3 crashed into a home in Katy, Texas, on Friday night, killing 76-year-old Martha Avila after she was taken to a hospital. Driver Michael Butler told Harris County sheriff's deputies that Autopilot was in use at the time of the crash. Tesla AI software vice president Ashok Elluswamy said on X that vehicle data showed the driver pressed the accelerator to 100% in the residential area, reached 73 mph, and kept the pedal depressed after impact. Elon Musk also said the company's Full Self-Driving system would not normally travel at that speed on neighborhood streets. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said it is opening a special investigation into the crash, and the Harris County Sheriff's Office said it will present its findings to the local district attorney to determine whether charges are warranted. Investigators have not yet released a final determination on whether a Tesla driver-assistance system was active, overridden, or malfunctioning.

London’s Metropolitan Police plans to expand its use of live facial recognition cameras, starting in the West End and Soho by December and extending to six more areas in 2027, according to The Guardian.

articles.content:
London’s Metropolitan Police plans to expand its use of live facial recognition cameras, starting in the West End and Soho by December and extending to six more areas in 2027, according to The Guardian.

The report said the force intends to use fixed cameras that could be attached to street furniture such as lamp posts. The system scans faces of people passing by and compares them with watchlists of wanted suspects. The Met said any arrest following an alert would still be made by a human officer.

The article said the force has been trialing the technology, including a pilot in Croydon using static cameras. The Met said 173 arrests were made during a six-month Croydon pilot and that one person was wrongly identified among 470,000 faces scanned.

Met Commissioner Mark Rowley said the technology works and has public support. Critics, including Big Brother Watch director Silkie Carlo, said expanding the system would subject large numbers of people to surveillance despite not being suspected of wrongdoing.

The Met said deployments would be advertised in advance and that images of people not matched to suspects would be deleted almost immediately.

articles.content:
Nvidia said its Rubin-generation reference design for data centers could reduce water use to near zero by relying on full liquid cooling and operating servers at higher temperatures.

According to The Verge, Nvidia said the system captures heat directly at the chip and moves it through liquid loops running at higher temperatures, allowing outdoor dry coolers to reject heat efficiently in many conditions. The article said Nvidia reported server temperatures could reach as high as 113 degrees Fahrenheit, or 45 degrees Celsius.

Josh Parker, Nvidia’s head of sustainability, said the design could reduce water use from about 2.6 million gallons per megawatt per year for conventional cooling-tower systems to near zero. The Verge noted that the claim does not address all concerns around AI data centers, including construction impacts, power demand and building costs.

The company said it is working with Trail of Bits on the effort and that security staff from Trail of Bits will work directly with maintainers to review potential issues using OpenAI tools including Codex Security. OpenAI said the program is designed to reduce the burden on maintainers by reviewing findings before they are sent on and by helpi
articles.content:
OpenAI said it has launched Patch the Planet, an initiative intended to help open-source maintainers identify, review and patch software vulnerabilities.

The company said it is working with Trail of Bits on the effort and that security staff from Trail of Bits will work directly with maintainers to review potential issues using OpenAI tools including Codex Security. OpenAI said the program is designed to reduce the burden on maintainers by reviewing findings before they are sent on and by helping develop patches and tests.

The article said open-source software underpins much of the commercial software industry and that vulnerabilities in widely used projects can have broad downstream effects. It cited the Log4j vulnerability as an example of how flaws in open-source components can affect other systems.

TechCrunch said it remains unclear how the initiative will scale over time, but described it as an effort to use AI systems in support of defensive software security work.

The article said the tool scans dependencies locally and can identify cases where override entries no longer affect the resolved package tree. Sonu Kapoor, the tool’s creator, told The Register that such issues can arise when projects change package managers or when older security-related overrides rem
articles.content:
An open-source tool called CVE Lite CLI has added an override auditing feature aimed at helping developers find ineffective dependency overrides in JavaScript projects, according to The Register.

The article said the tool scans dependencies locally and can identify cases where override entries no longer affect the resolved package tree. Sonu Kapoor, the tool’s creator, told The Register that such issues can arise when projects change package managers or when older security-related overrides remain in configuration files after they stop working.

Kapoor said he tested the feature on four open-source JavaScript projects and found broken overrides in three of them, while one project produced no findings. He said package managers look for overrides in different configuration locations, which can leave intended protections inactive without a warning.

The article also said AI coding assistants often advise developers to add overrides to address vulnerable transitive dependencies, but Kapoor argued that teams should later verify whether those overrides still function as intended.

A blog post highlighted memcached as a preferred caching tool for some infrastructure use cases, arguing that its simpler design can make operations easier than Redis when teams need a cache rather than a general-purpose data store.

The author said Redis is often introduced as a cache but can end up being used like a database, which can create operational and monitoring problems if teams assume the stored data is disposable. The post argued that by contrast, memcached’s lack of disk persistence
articles.content:
A blog post highlighted memcached as a preferred caching tool for some infrastructure use cases, arguing that its simpler design can make operations easier than Redis when teams need a cache rather than a general-purpose data store.

The author said Redis is often introduced as a cache but can end up being used like a database, which can create operational and monitoring problems if teams assume the stored data is disposable. The post argued that by contrast, memcached’s lack of disk persistence can make its role clearer and reduce the risk of it being treated as permanent storage.

The essay also said memcached clients often handle downtime by returning default values and that distributing workloads across multiple memcached nodes can be managed at the client level through hashing rather than built-in clustering.

The post concluded that many performance problems attributed to slow databases may instead be caused by inefficient queries or missing indexes.

The rep
articles.content:
A number of technology companies have announced layoffs in 2026 while citing artificial intelligence adoption, infrastructure spending or organizational restructuring as factors in the cuts.

TechCrunch reported that Oracle disclosed in an annual filing on June 22 that its workforce declined by 21,000 employees over the previous 12 months, a 13% reduction. The company said the adoption and deployment of AI technologies had resulted, and could continue to result, in workforce reductions.

The report also listed other companies that said AI played a role in staffing changes, including GitLab, Intuit, Meta, Cisco, Cloudflare, Coinbase, PayPal, Microsoft, Snap, IBM, Atlassian, Dell, Block, Salesforce and Amazon. In some cases, companies described the cuts as part of efforts to simplify management structures, redirect spending toward AI products or infrastructure, or change how work is organized.

Some companies in the list did not provide a single total for layoffs, while others announced specific percentages or headcount reductions. The article also noted that several of the companies reported revenue growth while reducing staff.

OpenAI said it is expanding its Daybreak cybersecurity program with an updated Codex Security plugin, a broader release of GPT-5.5-Cyber to approved defenders, a new cyber partner program, and an open-source security initiative called Patch the Planet.

According to OpenAI, GPT-5.5-Cyber is designed for authorized cybersecurity work and improved on GPT-5.5 in the company’s reported benchmark results, including CyberGym, ExploitGym and SEC-bench Pro. OpenAI said the model is intended to help defe
articles.content:
OpenAI said it is expanding its Daybreak cybersecurity program with an updated Codex Security plugin, a broader release of GPT-5.5-Cyber to approved defenders, a new cyber partner program, and an open-source security initiative called Patch the Planet.

According to OpenAI, GPT-5.5-Cyber is designed for authorized cybersecurity work and improved on GPT-5.5 in the company’s reported benchmark results, including CyberGym, ExploitGym and SEC-bench Pro. OpenAI said the model is intended to help defenders identify, validate and patch software vulnerabilities across large codebases.

The company also said its Codex Security cloud preview has scanned more than 30 million commits across more than 30,000 codebases since March. OpenAI said human reviewers have marked more than 70,000 findings as fixed and that more than 500,000 findings were automatically determined to be fixed.

OpenAI said the Patch the Planet initiative, developed with Trail of Bits and in collaboration with HackerOne and Calif, aims to help open-source maintainers move from vulnerability reports to tested fixes. The company said more than 30 open-source projects have committed to participate, including cURL, Go, Python, Sigstore and pyca/cryptography.

OpenAI also said it is working with governments, critical infrastructure operators and security partners to expand access to defensive cybersecurity tools under additional controls.

articles.content:
Apple executive Eddy Cue received the 2026 Entertainment Person of the Year award at the Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity on June 22. Cannes Lions CEO Simon Cook said when the award was announced last month that Cue was being recognized for his role in Apple’s entertainment and storytelling efforts. In his acceptance remarks, Cue said Apple focuses on quality and on supporting storytellers through Apple TV.

Apple said in a press release that Cue oversees services including Apple TV, Apple Music, Apple Podcasts, Apple Books, Apple Pay, Apple News, Apple Fitness+, Apple Card, Apple Maps and iCloud, along with the company’s productivity and creativity apps.

During the earlier keynote, Cue and Bruckheimer said they hope to make another Formula 1 film. They also discussed an upcoming Apple TV project described by Bruckheimer as a conspiracy thriller involving unidentified anomalous phenomena.

articles.summary:
Researchers have released a technical report describing VibeThinker-3B, a 3 billion-parameter dense model built to test how far reasoning performance can be improved in smaller systems. The paper says the model was trained with curriculum-based supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning across multiple domains and offline self-distillation.

According to the report, VibeThinker-3B scored 94.3 on AIME 2026 and 80.2 Pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6, with higher results on some evaluations when addit
articles.content:
Researchers have released a technical report describing VibeThinker-3B, a 3 billion-parameter dense model built to test how far reasoning performance can be improved in smaller systems. The paper says the model was trained with curriculum-based supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning across multiple domains and offline self-distillation.

According to the report, VibeThinker-3B scored 94.3 on AIME 2026 and 80.2 Pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6, with higher results on some evaluations when additional test-time scaling was used. The authors also reported a 93.4 score on IFEval and said the model maintained instruction-following performance while improving on reasoning tasks.

The paper argues that some forms of verifiable reasoning may be compressed into relatively small models, while broader world knowledge and general-purpose capability may require much larger parameter counts. The authors describe that idea as a hypothesis for future research rather than a settled conclusion.

The work was posted on arXiv on June 15, 2026, and has not yet undergone peer review.

Omdia reported that India shipped 4.4 million PCs, including 3.5 million laptops, up 32 percent from a year earlier. The firm said vendors and channels increased inventory ahead of expected price increases, contributing to a 43 percent rise in consumer dema
articles.content:
China and India accounted for 13.1 million PC shipments in the first quarter of 2026, according to market research firm Omdia. The total represents about 20 percent of the 68.44 million PCs the firm said shipped worldwide during the quarter.

Omdia reported that India shipped 4.4 million PCs, including 3.5 million laptops, up 32 percent from a year earlier. The firm said vendors and channels increased inventory ahead of expected price increases, contributing to a 43 percent rise in consumer demand. In China, shipments totaled 8.9 million units, down 2 percent year over year. Omdia attributed part of the decline to the end of consumer subsidies.

The firm forecasts that China's PC shipments will fall 14 percent in 2026 and that India's will decline 5.3 percent for the full year, citing rising component costs.

The same Register report also said OpenAI announced a companywide Samsung Electronics deployment of ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex tools for employees in South Korea and for Device eXperience staff worldwide. It also reported that Reliance Jio is evaluating a low Earth orbit satellite broadband network and that Vietnam plans to help develop 10 domestic technology companies with $1 billion in revenue by 2030.

A software developer has published an informal benchmark designed to test whether publicly available AI models can identify difficult security vulnerabilities that Anthropic has said were found by its Mythos system. The benchmark uses nine known bugs drawn from cases described in Anthropic materials and tests whether other models can detect and explain them without being told what flaw to look for.

According to the author, each case starts from code that predates the bug fix, with human spot ch
articles.content:
A software developer has published an informal benchmark designed to test whether publicly available AI models can identify difficult security vulnerabilities that Anthropic has said were found by its Mythos system. The benchmark uses nine known bugs drawn from cases described in Anthropic materials and tests whether other models can detect and explain them without being told what flaw to look for.

According to the author, each case starts from code that predates the bug fix, with human spot checks and model-assisted review used to confirm that the vulnerability is real and understandable when the model is directed to the relevant area. The author said the current dataset is small and that results are based on one run per model for each bug.

The published results suggest some lower-cost and open models identified several of the target bugs, while no model matched Mythos on every case. The author wrote that four bugs reported to have been found by Mythos were not found by any model in the experiment.

The post describes the work as preliminary rather than conclusive and says prompt design, tooling and repeated runs could change outcomes. It also argues that current public models may be capable of stronger performance with better setups and more time.

According to FAA documents cited by Ars Technica, Starfall is a cylindrical vehicle about 10.2 feet in diameter and 2.5 feet high. It weighs about 4,600 pounds and can carry roughly 2,200 pound
articles.content:
The Federal Aviation Administration has approved SpaceX plans for two Starfall reentry demonstrations, clearing the company to test a cargo-focused vehicle designed to return payloads from orbit. The agency did not specify whether the two demonstrations would occur on one mission or two separate flights.

According to FAA documents cited by Ars Technica, Starfall is a cylindrical vehicle about 10.2 feet in diameter and 2.5 feet high. It weighs about 4,600 pounds and can carry roughly 2,200 pounds of cargo, for a total loaded weight of about 6,800 pounds. SpaceX plans to recover the vehicle, including parachutes and heat-shield components, as much as practicable.

The first demonstration is expected to spend several hours in low Earth orbit, though the vehicle could also fly on shorter suborbital missions atop Falcon 9 or Starship. This version of Starfall cannot de-orbit on its own and instead depends on its launch vehicle to guide reentry before using compressed nitrogen to orient its heat shield.

Ars Technica reported that the U.S. military is a likely customer because the Pentagon has been studying rapid global cargo delivery from space. Other companies, including Blue Origin, Rocket Lab and Anduril, are also working with the military on related concepts.

The agencies said cybersecurity should be treated as a business risk rather than only a
articles.content:
Leaders of intelligence agencies from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom and the United States issued joint guidance urging organizations to strengthen basic cybersecurity practices as AI tools increase the speed and scale of cyber threats. The guidance says assumptions about cyber risk may become outdated within months as advanced AI systems improve both offensive and defensive capabilities.

The agencies said cybersecurity should be treated as a business risk rather than only a technical issue. They recommended that organizations assess risk and accountability, prioritize core security controls, give cyber leaders sufficient authority and resources, and remain engaged as threats and guidance change.

The document also lists five immediate actions: reduce external exposure of systems, speed up patching, address unsupported legacy systems, strengthen identity and access controls, and test incident response plans. The agencies said organizations should assume breaches will occur and focus on rapid containment and recovery.

The guidance also says AI can be used to improve defenses, including earlier detection of vulnerabilities, software quality monitoring and faster incident response.

ESA astronaut Sophie Adenot tested a European intravehicular activity spacesuit prototype aboard the International Space Station, while a second prototype underwent water-survival testing in Marseille.

The EuroSuit project began in December 2023 under the French space agency CNES. Development is being led by a consortium that includes Spartan Space, the Institute of Space Medicine and Physiology, known as MEDES, and Decathlon. An early prototype was sent to the station in May 2026 on SpaceX’s 3
articles.content:
ESA astronaut Sophie Adenot tested a European intravehicular activity spacesuit prototype aboard the International Space Station, while a second prototype underwent water-survival testing in Marseille.

The EuroSuit project began in December 2023 under the French space agency CNES. Development is being led by a consortium that includes Spartan Space, the Institute of Space Medicine and Physiology, known as MEDES, and Decathlon. An early prototype was sent to the station in May 2026 on SpaceX’s 34th Commercial Resupply Services mission for NASA.

NASA’s June 11 operations update said Adenot tested how quickly the suit could be put on, with a target time of two minutes or less, and also evaluated mobility while wearing it. After the session, she recorded feedback on timing, comfort, ergonomics and how easily she could handle objects in the suit.

European Spaceflight reported that this appeared to be the second of three planned test sessions. A separate NASA update from May 29 referred to a prototype internal spacesuit without naming EuroSuit directly. Once station testing is complete, NASA said the suit will be discarded.

On June 12, Spartan Space said it completed a water-survival test campaign in Marseille covering buoyancy, emergency egress, life raft boarding and recovery scenarios. The company said the results would support the next stage of qualification. Under its current CNES contract, the consortium is expected to complete a final ground version of the suit by the end of 2027 as a precursor to a flight version.

Astroscale said it is moving from technology demonstrations toward regular on-orbit servicing missions, supported by new funding and higher project income.

The Tokyo-based company reported 11.5 billion yen, or about $71.1 million, in project income for its fiscal year ended April 30, nearly double the 6.1 billion yen reported a year earlier. It reported an operating loss of 10 billion yen, improved from 18.8 billion yen in the previous fiscal year.

For the current fiscal year, Astroscale proje
articles.content:
Astroscale said it is moving from technology demonstrations toward regular on-orbit servicing missions, supported by new funding and higher project income.

The Tokyo-based company reported 11.5 billion yen, or about $71.1 million, in project income for its fiscal year ended April 30, nearly double the 6.1 billion yen reported a year earlier. It reported an operating loss of 10 billion yen, improved from 18.8 billion yen in the previous fiscal year.

For the current fiscal year, Astroscale projects project income of 12.5 billion yen to 17 billion yen and expects an operating loss of 9 billion yen to 9.9 billion yen. Chief executive Nobu Okada said the company plans to continue investing in talent, development and production while it works to secure repeat business.

Astroscale has missions in development in Japan, the United Kingdom and the United States, including spacecraft refueling and satellite end-of-life disposal demonstrations scheduled over the next two years. The company said demand from defense customers has increased in areas including space domain awareness and refueling, while it is also discussing future life-extension services for commercial satellites.

In May, Astroscale announced that it raised 30.6 billion yen through convertible bonds and equity. The company said it plans to use part of the funding to expand production facilities in Japan and the United Kingdom, manufacture its LEXI-P spacecraft and support working capital.

The article says the United States has historically had stronger influence in Africa through aid than through foreign direct investment, while China has expanded its presence through trade and infrastructure investment. It argues that Washington should focus on sectors where it still has a competitive a
articles.content:
A SpaceNews opinion article by Philip Dackiw argues that the United States should place greater emphasis on Africa’s developing space sector as part of its broader engagement with the continent.

The article says the United States has historically had stronger influence in Africa through aid than through foreign direct investment, while China has expanded its presence through trade and infrastructure investment. It argues that Washington should focus on sectors where it still has a competitive advantage, including space.

According to the article, more than 20 African countries now have space agencies or research centers supported by national laws, and the African Space Agency was established in April 2025. The author describes satellite services, satellite development facilities and, eventually, launch infrastructure as possible areas for U.S. investment.

The piece concludes that stronger commercial partnerships in Africa’s space industry could help deepen U.S. ties on the continent while increasing competition with China. The views are the author’s and were published by SpaceNews as an opinion submission.

articles.headline: Rocket Lab launches spacecraft for U.S. Space Force responsive-space exercise
articles.deck: Rocket Lab launched the Victus Haze Puma spacecraft for the U.S.
articles.summary:
Rocket Lab launched the Victus Haze Puma spacecraft for the U.S. Space Force on June 19 from Mahia, New Zealand, aboard an Electron rocket. The satellite was developed under a $32 million Space Force contract awarded in 2024 and is one of two spacecraft involved in the Victus Haze mission.

The spacecraft was inserted into sun-synchronous orbit and is to undergo commissioning before beginning rendezvous and proximity operations with a spacecraft operated by True Anomaly. Space Systems Command sa
articles.content:
Rocket Lab launched the Victus Haze Puma spacecraft for the U.S. Space Force on June 19 from Mahia, New Zealand, aboard an Electron rocket. The satellite was developed under a $32 million Space Force contract awarded in 2024 and is one of two spacecraft involved in the Victus Haze mission.

The spacecraft was inserted into sun-synchronous orbit and is to undergo commissioning before beginning rendezvous and proximity operations with a spacecraft operated by True Anomaly. Space Systems Command said June 22 that after on-orbit checkout and commissioning, the two spacecraft will conduct scenarios intended to demonstrate space domain awareness and characterization capabilities.

Victus Haze is the fourth demonstration under the Space Force’s Tactically Responsive Space program. According to the Space Force and Rocket Lab, the mission is intended to show that commercial providers can launch spacecraft on short timelines and begin operations quickly once on orbit.

Rocket Lab said it launched within 16 hours and 42 minutes of receiving the formal launch order, beating a 24-hour mission requirement. The company also said its spacecraft completed commissioning in 37 hours and 36 minutes, ahead of the mission’s 72-hour operational deadline.

True Anomaly previously received a $30 million contract for Victus Haze. Its Jackal-004 spacecraft, launched May 3 on a SpaceX Falcon 9 rideshare mission, is expected to maneuver with Rocket Lab’s vehicle during the exercise.

articles.content:
NASA said its Wallops Flight Facility in Virginia is scheduled to launch a sounding rocket carrying student-developed experiments on Wednesday, June 24, during a launch window from 5:30 a.m. to 9:30 a.m. EDT, with June 25 available as a backup day.

The agency said the mission combines its RockSatX and RockOn programs on a single rocket for the first time. The flight will carry nearly 50 experiments built by about 250 participants from 38 university and community college teams.

NASA said the student payloads include experiments measuring weather and radiation in the upper atmosphere and testing technologies such as heat shields, space-debris tracking and robotic servicing. The Terrier-Improved Malemute sounding rocket is expected to reach an altitude of about 100 miles before descending by parachute into the Atlantic Ocean for recovery.

NASA said the Wallops Visitor Center viewing area will open at 4:30 a.m. on launch day and that a livestream will begin about 10 minutes before liftoff on the Wallops YouTube channel. The agency said the program is intended to provide technical training and mission experience for students entering the aerospace field.

NASA said June 22 that it will begin processing multiple contract awards under its Solutions for Enterprise-wide Procurement VI government-wide acquisition program.

The agency said the contracts provide access to commercial products and services including hardware, software, cloud services, cybersecurity tools, engineering and consulting services, and data-intensive mission support capabilities. The competition was conducted across three categories: IT solutions, enterprise-wide IT service solutions, and IT mission-based services.

NASA said all awards are indefinite-delivery, indefinite-quantity contracts that can support several pricing arrangements, including firm-fixed-price, labor-hour and time-and-materials task orders. The ordering period runs for 10 years, from Nov. 1 through Oct. 31, 2036, and each contract carries a maximum value of $20 billion.

The agency said a full list of awardees and additional program information is available through the SEWP program website.

articles.summary:
President Donald Trump signed an executive order June 22 directing NASA to submit within 120 days a five-year plan for developing civilian quantum sensing and networking for space applications, SpaceNews reported.

The order is part of a broader federal effort to accelerate U.S. quantum technology development. According to the report, other agencies were also instructed to produce five-year plans, while the Department of War was directed to identify at least three next-generation quantum sensor
articles.content:
President Donald Trump signed an executive order June 22 directing NASA to submit within 120 days a five-year plan for developing civilian quantum sensing and networking for space applications, SpaceNews reported.

The order is part of a broader federal effort to accelerate U.S. quantum technology development. According to the report, other agencies were also instructed to produce five-year plans, while the Department of War was directed to identify at least three next-generation quantum sensor projects within 60 days for prioritization through Sept. 30, 2028. A separate executive order addresses cryptographic protections against risks posed by future quantum computers.

SpaceNews also reported that quantum technology company Infleqtion announced an industry coalition called America’s Quantum Space Initiative shortly before the order was signed. Founding partners include Voyager Technologies, Armada, Monarch Quantum and the University of Colorado Boulder.

Infleqtion Chief Executive Matt Kinsella said the company has worked with NASA and the Jet Propulsion Laboratory for about a decade and aims to deploy a quantum gravity gradiometer with NASA and JPL before the end of the decade. He said likely early space applications include sensing, secure communications and advanced atomic clocks, while quantum computing in space is expected to take longer.

articles.content:
SpaceX is scheduled to launch an uncrewed demonstration mission for a new reentry capsule called Starfall aboard a Falcon 9 rocket from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station on June 23, according to Spaceflight Now.

The report said liftoff was scheduled for 6:43 a.m. EDT from Space Launch Complex 40, with a 95 percent chance of favorable weather forecast by the 45th Weather Squadron. The mission is set to use Falcon 9 booster B1078, which would attempt a landing on the drone ship A Shortfall of Gravitas after launch.

SpaceX has released limited public information about Starfall. Spaceflight Now reported that a Federal Aviation Administration environmental assessment said SpaceX sought to perform two Starfall reentries to demonstrate future cargo transport and in-space manufacturing capabilities, though it was not clear how many capsules were onboard this flight.

According to the environmental assessment cited in the report, each capsule is about 0.75 meters tall, 3.1 meters in diameter and can carry up to 1,000 kilograms of payload. The document says the capsules are designed for ocean recovery in the Pacific and use inert gas for attitude control rather than a main propulsion system. The filing also describes Starfall as a potential platform for returning cargo from orbit and supporting commercial microgravity manufacturing.

articles.headline: NASA and Boeing still reviewing schedule for next Starliner test flight
articles.deck: NASA and Boeing are continuing work on technical and organizational issues affecting the CST-100 Starliner spacecraft, but the timing of its next flight remains uncertain, Space...
articles.summary:
NASA and Boeing are continuing work on technical and organizational issues affecting the CST-100 Starliner spacecraft, but the timing of its next flight remains uncertain, SpaceNews reported June 23.

Members of NASA’s Aerospace Safety Advisory Panel said June 22 that the next mission, an uncrewed test flight called Starliner-1, does not yet have a formal launch date and could still be as much as about a year away. The spacecraft has not flown since the June 2024 Crew Flight Test, when thruster
articles.content:
NASA and Boeing are continuing work on technical and organizational issues affecting the CST-100 Starliner spacecraft, but the timing of its next flight remains uncertain, SpaceNews reported June 23.

Members of NASA’s Aerospace Safety Advisory Panel said June 22 that the next mission, an uncrewed test flight called Starliner-1, does not yet have a formal launch date and could still be as much as about a year away. The spacecraft has not flown since the June 2024 Crew Flight Test, when thruster failures and other problems occurred during its approach to the International Space Station. NASA later determined the vehicle should return without crew, while astronauts Suni Williams and Butch Wilmore remained on the station until returning on a Crew Dragon in March 2025.

According to the report, NASA and Boeing have made progress in addressing observations and in-flight anomalies from the mission. However, some key propulsion issues remain, including reaction control system thruster failures and heating in the service module thruster enclosures known as doghouses.

The advisory panel also cited changes in management and closer coordination between NASA and Boeing. At the same time, NASA is preparing for the possibility of a longer delay by seeking additional SpaceX commercial crew flights, which the agency said would support International Space Station operations through its planned retirement in 2030.

articles.headline: NASA inspector general report details launch infrastructure needs at Kennedy and Wallops
articles.deck: A report from NASA’s Office of Inspector General says aging launch infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility requires major investment as launch demand ...
articles.summary:
A report from NASA’s Office of Inspector General says aging launch infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility requires major investment as launch demand increases, according to coverage published June 22 by SpacePolicyOnline.com.

The report says many launch pads and supporting systems at Kennedy date to the 1960s, while infrastructure at Wallops is older. It also cites deteriorating roads, bridges and utility systems used for launch operations. NASA officials estimate Ke
articles.content:
A report from NASA’s Office of Inspector General says aging launch infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center and Wallops Flight Facility requires major investment as launch demand increases, according to coverage published June 22 by SpacePolicyOnline.com.

The report says many launch pads and supporting systems at Kennedy date to the 1960s, while infrastructure at Wallops is older. It also cites deteriorating roads, bridges and utility systems used for launch operations. NASA officials estimate Kennedy alone needs at least $1 billion in improvements, the report says, although Congress previously provided $250 million for launch infrastructure work there.

One recommendation is for Congress to authorize NASA to accept financial contributions from commercial partners for capital infrastructure projects. SpacePolicyOnline.com reported that the Anti-Deficiency Act currently prevents NASA from accepting such funds as an addition to appropriated money and restricts the agency from paying for projects that mainly benefit commercial users.

The report also recommends that NASA rely more on Enhanced Use Lease agreements, which can recover fair market value for land and infrastructure use, instead of Commercial Space Launch Act agreements that recover only direct launch and reentry service costs. The broader finding, according to the report, is that NASA’s infrastructure needs are rising while construction and maintenance budgets are declining.

articles.headline: Satellite data show Lake Naivasha has expanded as rainfall increases
articles.deck: NASA Earth Observatory reported June 23 that Kenya’s Lake Naivasha has risen sharply over the past 25 years, with satellite altimetry showing an increase of about 7 meters since...
articles.summary:
NASA Earth Observatory reported June 23 that Kenya’s Lake Naivasha has risen sharply over the past 25 years, with satellite altimetry showing an increase of about 7 meters since 2010 and Landsat data indicating the lake’s area expanded by roughly 40 percent, or about 50 square kilometers.

According to the report, the higher water levels have flooded homes, roads, farms and other infrastructure along the lakeshore, including parts of the town of Naivasha and the neighborhood of Kihoto. The chang
articles.content:
NASA Earth Observatory reported June 23 that Kenya’s Lake Naivasha has risen sharply over the past 25 years, with satellite altimetry showing an increase of about 7 meters since 2010 and Landsat data indicating the lake’s area expanded by roughly 40 percent, or about 50 square kilometers.

According to the report, the higher water levels have flooded homes, roads, farms and other infrastructure along the lakeshore, including parts of the town of Naivasha and the neighborhood of Kihoto. The changes have also effectively merged Lake Oloidien with Naivasha, introducing more saline and alkaline water into Naivasha’s freshwater system.

Hydrologist Mathew Herrnegger said increased rainfall is the main driver. He said mean annual rainfall rose about 30 percent between 2010 and 2020 compared with the previous decade, alongside a sharp increase in high-intensity rainfall. Because the lake sits in a closed basin with no surface outflow, researchers said relatively small changes in rainfall can produce large changes in water levels.

The report said the lake’s expansion has affected the regional flower industry, wildlife tourism and fishing. It also noted that researchers are examining other possible contributing factors, including groundwater recharge, tectonic influences, sediment accumulation and the spread of aquatic vegetation such as water hyacinth.

The European Space Agency on June 23 published a roundup of open opportunities tied to its satellite navigation programs, including Galileo, EGNOS, NAVISP, FutureNAV, Celeste, Genesis and Moonlight. The notice covers invitations to tender, calls for proposals, industry events and education programs for European citizens, companies, researchers and students.

Among the listed procurement opportunities, ESA said the Celeste In-Orbit Preparatory program is intended to prepare European industry for a possible future low Earth orbit positioning, navigation and timing system for the European Union. The related tender closes June 28, 2026, and is open to entities in a defined list of participating countries. ESA also said third parties from participating states can submit ongoing proposals to take part in the experimentation phase of the Celeste In-Orbit Demonstrator.

ESA said it is also soliciting work on OpSTAR, an in-orbit demonstration mission focused on optical technologies for satellite navigation, with a closing date of July 8, 2026. Additional FutureNAV opportunities include an ongoing call for ideas on future PNT demonstrators and two studies on AI-enabled digital payloads and quantum-enabled PNT demonstrators, both extended to Aug. 17, 2026.

The agency also highlighted ongoing NAVISP proposal channels, the NAVISP Industry Days event scheduled for Oct. 14-16 in Rome, the 10th GNSS Science Colloquium in Montreal on Oct. 7-9, and education programs whose next student application windows open in 2027. ESA said current vacancies are also posted through its regular careers portal.

articles.headline: Americans Split on Legality of Political Prediction Markets
articles.deck: A POLITICO poll conducted by Public First found that 44 percent of U.S.
articles.summary:
A POLITICO poll conducted by Public First found that 44 percent of U.S. adults say betting on election outcomes should be illegal. Similar shares said wagers on what the president or other public figures will say, and on who may receive a presidential pardon, should also be illegal.

The survey comes as prediction-market companies including Kalshi and Polymarket expand offerings tied to politics, sports and other events. More than half of respondents said they would not consider placing a bet on
articles.content:
A POLITICO poll conducted by Public First found that 44 percent of U.S. adults say betting on election outcomes should be illegal. Similar shares said wagers on what the president or other public figures will say, and on who may receive a presidential pardon, should also be illegal.

The survey comes as prediction-market companies including Kalshi and Polymarket expand offerings tied to politics, sports and other events. More than half of respondents said they would not consider placing a bet on a prediction market, according to the poll. Younger adults showed greater interest: 12 percent of respondents ages 18 to 24 and 25 to 34 said they had placed a prediction-market wager, compared with 6 percent overall.

Supporters of political prediction markets say the products provide real-time information and can help people and businesses gauge the likely effects of elections or legislation. John Aristotle Phillips of PredictIt said some people will accept certain markets while objecting to others. Caleb Davies, a prediction-market trader in Minnesota, said markets can offer information beyond polling and commentary.

Critics say political betting could create risks for elections and governance. Sen. Jeff Merkley, an Oregon Democrat, said large bets combined with political spending could affect election outcomes. The poll also found that a majority of respondents believe wagers on war outcomes and terrorist acts should be illegal.

Political prediction markets expanded after a federal judge ruled in 2024 against Commodity Futures Trading Commission objections to Kalshi offering election contracts. Since then, trading on political events has grown, including markets on the 2028 presidential election, Cabinet officials and legislation. Bloomberg Intelligence analysts said this month that politics and public-policy markets could become a major growth area for companies such as Kalshi and Polymarket.

The industry also faces regulatory and legal scrutiny. States, tribal groups and gambling-industry interests have argued that prediction-market firms are avoiding sports-betting rules, which the companies and the CFTC dispute. The poll was conducted weeks after the Justice Department and the CFTC charged a U.S. soldier with using confidential information to trade on Polymarket in a case involving the capture of then-Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro.

Espaillat, who has represented the di
articles.content:
Darializa Avila Chevalier, a progressive Democrat endorsed by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, is challenging Rep. Adriano Espaillat in the Democratic primary for New York’s 13th Congressional District.

Avila Chevalier told The Guardian that she is running because she believes the district needs new representation focused on working people. She criticized Espaillat’s record and said conditions in the district have not improved during his time in office.

Espaillat, who has represented the district since 2017 and previously held other elected offices, said at a WNYC candidate forum that Avila Chevalier lacked the experience needed for Congress. The district includes parts of upper Manhattan and the Bronx and has a large Afro-Latino population.

Mamdani has endorsed Avila Chevalier and two other congressional candidates in New York City. Avila Chevalier, 32, previously worked as an organizing lead in Mamdani’s mayoral campaign for her region. She currently works as a public defense investigator, is completing a PhD at the City University of New York and has support from UAW Region 9A.

Her platform includes universal healthcare, campaign finance reform and abolishing Immigration and Customs Enforcement, according to the article.

articles.headline: Judge blocks subpoenas for Minnesota officials in immigration inquiry
articles.deck: A federal judge blocked subpoenas issued to Minnesota Gov.
articles.summary:
A federal judge blocked subpoenas issued to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz and other state and local officials as part of a federal investigation tied to immigration enforcement in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area.

In a ruling unsealed Monday, U.S. District Judge Patrick Schiltz said the subpoenas appeared aimed at pressuring Minnesota officials to assist with federal civil immigration enforcement and at retaliating against them for not doing so. He wrote that the connection between the requested records
articles.content:
A federal judge blocked subpoenas issued to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz and other state and local officials as part of a federal investigation tied to immigration enforcement in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area.

In a ruling unsealed Monday, U.S. District Judge Patrick Schiltz said the subpoenas appeared aimed at pressuring Minnesota officials to assist with federal civil immigration enforcement and at retaliating against them for not doing so. He wrote that the connection between the requested records and any possible criminal violation was "extremely weak to nonexistent" and said the materials sought largely concerned constitutionally protected conduct.

The subpoenas were served in January to the offices of Walz, Attorney General Keith Ellison, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey, St. Paul Mayor Kaohly Her, and officials in Ramsey and Hennepin counties. They were part of an investigation into whether officials had obstructed or impeded law enforcement during a broad immigration operation.

Schiltz wrote that Minnesota is not required to use its own resources to enforce federal immigration law. The Justice Department said it takes possible obstruction of federal law enforcement operations seriously and would continue to investigate such matters in compliance with the law.

Walz called the ruling a victory for the rule of law, while Ellison, Her and Frey criticized the investigation in separate statements.

Chief operating officer Therrin Protze said the complex also plans to mark the 60th anniversary of its bus tours in July with commemorative decals, themed photo opportunities and souvenir popcorn containers.

The article says a larger expansion announcement is planned for November at the International Association of Amusement Parks and Attractions Expo in Orlando. It also says the recent Artemis II mission drew 350,000 visitors to the Space Coast on launch day and has contributed to increased attendance at the visitor complex.

articles.summary:
AAA said Florida gas prices fell by about 32 cents per gallon on average over 11 days ahead of the Independence Day travel period.

According to the report, the statewide average on Monday was $3.61 per gallon, compared with a national average of about $3.93. AAA spokesperson Mark Jenkins said the lower prices could help summer travelers, though he said developments involving the Strait of Hormuz could still affect prices.

The article says West Palm Beach had some of the highest prices in the s
articles.content:
AAA said Florida gas prices fell by about 32 cents per gallon on average over 11 days ahead of the Independence Day travel period.

According to the report, the statewide average on Monday was $3.61 per gallon, compared with a national average of about $3.93. AAA spokesperson Mark Jenkins said the lower prices could help summer travelers, though he said developments involving the Strait of Hormuz could still affect prices.

The article says West Palm Beach had some of the highest prices in the state at about $3.82 per gallon, followed by Miami at $3.75 and Fort Lauderdale at $3.71. Pensacola had some of the lowest prices at about $3.41, followed by the Space Coast at $3.48 and Fort Walton Beach at $3.50.

AAA projected that 4.65 million Floridians would travel during the holiday period, including 4.08 million by car. It said 72.2 million Americans were expected to travel overall.

articles.summary:
A federal judge quashed subpoenas issued by the US Department of Justice to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, Attorney General Keith Ellison, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey and other officials during a federal immigration investigation.

According to the report, the subpoenas were issued in January as part of an investigation into whether state and local officials were obstructing federal immigration enforcement. The order, unsealed Monday by the US district court in Minnesota, said the subpoenas were issu
articles.content:
A federal judge quashed subpoenas issued by the US Department of Justice to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, Attorney General Keith Ellison, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey and other officials during a federal immigration investigation.

According to the report, the subpoenas were issued in January as part of an investigation into whether state and local officials were obstructing federal immigration enforcement. The order, unsealed Monday by the US district court in Minnesota, said the subpoenas were issued for unlawful reasons.

Chief Judge Patrick J. Schiltz wrote that the evidence the subpoenas were issued for unlawful reasons was overwhelming and that the department had not identified a plausible investigative justification. He concluded that the dominant purpose was to pressure Minnesota officials to assist with enforcement of civil immigration law and to retaliate against them for not doing so.

Ellison said the decision to quash the subpoenas was an unusual step by the court.

articles.content:
In a first-person Florida Politics column, publisher Peter Schorsch discusses online attacks directed at him by Jacob Engels and reflects on how those attacks affect him, his family and his readers.

Schorsch says he received alerts linking to material about him, including a long AI-produced video. He writes that the experience led him to think about how information about him online could affect his daughter in the future.

The column also recounts Schorsch’s past interactions with Engels and describes Engels’ legal and political history as Schorsch understands it. Schorsch says he initially wondered whether someone was financing the attacks but concluded that Engels was acting independently.

The article is written as commentary and centers on Schorsch’s personal account and views.

articles.content:
New campaign finance reports show St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch raised nearly $23,000 in his campaign account from April 1 through June 12, less than former Gov. Charlie Crist, City Council member Brandi Gabbard and former Fire Chief Jim Large during the same period.

Crist reported raising nearly $71,000 in his candidate account and spent about $15,500, leaving more than $55,000 on hand. Gabbard reported just over $32,000, including a $5,000 candidate loan, and spent nearly $19,000, leaving roughly $17,000 on hand. Large reported raising just over $29,000 and spending more than $19,000, leaving just over $65,000 on hand, a figure that includes a $30,000 candidate contribution made earlier in the race.

Welch spent more than $29,000 during the period and ended with about $19,000 in his campaign account. His political committee, St. Petersburg Progress, reported about $114,000 raised in its most recent filing and about $301,000 raised overall.

Crist’s political committee, St. Pete Shines, reported more than $282,500 raised from April through May and nearly $1.5 million since launching. Large’s committee, A Better Burg, reported $13,500 raised in the latest period and about $40,000 on hand after spending. Gabbard’s committee, Forward Together PC, had about $3,000 on hand.

Maria Scruggs and Kevin Batdorf reported much smaller totals. The primary is scheduled for August 18, with a November 3 runoff if no candidate wins more than 50%.

articles.content:
A Florida Politics opinion column by Peter Schorsch argues that St. Petersburg and Pinellas County are not well positioned to earn Michelin stars and should not make that goal a priority.

The column says Visit St. Pete-Clearwater paid $180,000 over two years to be eligible for Michelin consideration, yet no restaurant in Pinellas County received a star for a second straight year. It argues that Michelin inspectors place heavy weight on consistency and service, and says local restaurants often face challenges in both areas.

The piece cites examples of restaurants in other parts of Florida that received recognition, including restaurants in Jacksonville, St. Augustine, Tallahassee and Pensacola, and says the statewide Michelin arrangement brought attention to places that had not previously been considered. It also notes that some Tampa Bay restaurants have closed or lost recognition over time.

The article is presented as commentary and reflects the author’s assessment of the region’s restaurant scene.

articles.summary:
President Donald Trump said damage at the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool after a $14 million renovation was caused by vandalism, though he did not provide evidence. He told reporters that five people had been arrested and five others were under investigation.

Trump said the pool may need to be drained again to repair peeling paint and algae problems. He also said someone cut the coating and put fertilizer in the water. The Department of the Interior, the National Park Service and US Park Poli
articles.content:
President Donald Trump said damage at the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool after a $14 million renovation was caused by vandalism, though he did not provide evidence. He told reporters that five people had been arrested and five others were under investigation.

Trump said the pool may need to be drained again to repair peeling paint and algae problems. He also said someone cut the coating and put fertilizer in the water. The Department of the Interior, the National Park Service and US Park Police did not immediately comment, according to the report.

The renovation included painting the bottom of the pool a dark navy shade called “Old Glory Blue.” Trump said he awarded the work through a no-bid contract to a company that had previously worked on pools at one of his golf clubs. After the work was completed, algae appeared in the standing water and the paint coating began to detach.

A spokesperson for DC Water said the agency issued the National Park Service a temporary permit on June 16 to discharge water into a sewer connected to a treatment facility. The permit expires July 2.

articles.content:
HIV and AIDS activists in New York and around the country are protesting proposed federal policy changes they say could reverse decades of progress in treatment, prevention, and research. At a recent rally near the Stonewall Inn, participants marked 45 years since the first reported AIDS cases and objected to planned changes affecting Medicaid, global aid, and National Institutes of Health funding.

Advocates said new Medicaid work requirements could threaten coverage for many low-income people with HIV, while House proposals would cut $225 million from the Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program. The article also said several state drug assistance programs, including in Florida, are facing funding pressure.

Activists have also criticized delays and structural changes affecting international HIV programs previously administered through USAID and have pushed Congress to preserve research funding. Organizers said they are using both public demonstrations and direct lobbying to oppose the cuts.

articles.content:
Utah’s Republican primary in the 3rd Congressional District features incumbent Rep. Celeste Maloy and former state legislator Phil Lyman, two candidates with strong support from conservative voters. President Donald Trump recently endorsed Maloy, while he pardoned Lyman in 2020 over a conviction tied to an off-road vehicle protest on federal land.

Lyman became well known in Utah after organizing a 2014 protest in Recapture Canyon that led to misdemeanor convictions and a jail sentence. He later served in the state legislature and unsuccessfully ran for governor in 2024. Maloy, first elected in a 2023 special election, has support from much of Utah’s Republican establishment but has won recent primaries by narrow margins.

The article said the newly redrawn district could make the race more competitive, especially because many Republican voters there have not previously seen Maloy on the ballot. It also said Lyman has strong support among convention delegates and that turnout may determine the outcome.

articles.content:
An article on the Southern Baptist Convention said a group called the Center for Baptist Leadership has gained influence within the denomination as the SBC adopts more conservative positions on church governance, immigration, and public life. The SBC has more than 12.7 million members and about 46,000 churches, making it the largest Protestant denomination in the United States.

At its annual meeting in Orlando, delegates voted in favor of codifying a ban on women pastors, supported stronger immigration enforcement, and elected Florida pastor Willy Rice as president. The Center for Baptist Leadership backed Rice and has argued that the denomination should move away from recent social justice and abuse-accountability efforts.

The article linked the group’s leaders and advisers to Christian nationalist circles and said they have used podcasts, social media, and relationships with influential SBC figures to shape debate inside the denomination. It also noted that some religion scholars and reporters cautioned against overstating the group’s formal size while acknowledging its influence on the broader narrative.

articles.summary:
The U.S. Senate passed the 21st Century Road to Housing Act by an 85-5 vote, sending the bipartisan measure to the House of Representatives. The bill is intended to reduce housing costs by streamlining construction and permitting, expanding housing options, and limiting some investor home purchases.

Among its provisions, the bill would bar investors from buying single-family homes if they already own 350 or more properties, waive some federal permitting rules, support manufactured housing, and
articles.content:
The U.S. Senate passed the 21st Century Road to Housing Act by an 85-5 vote, sending the bipartisan measure to the House of Representatives. The bill is intended to reduce housing costs by streamlining construction and permitting, expanding housing options, and limiting some investor home purchases.

Among its provisions, the bill would bar investors from buying single-family homes if they already own 350 or more properties, waive some federal permitting rules, support manufactured housing, and expand mortgage availability. Lawmakers from both parties said the measure responds to high housing costs and a shortage of new construction.

Republican Sen. Tim Scott and Democratic Sen. Elizabeth Warren both praised the bill, while House Democrat Maxine Waters called it an important step but said more work remains on affordability. The legislation now moves to the House for consideration.

articles.content:

Judge Fernando Olguin rejected the administration’s argument that the policy was unconstitutional, while allowing the government to file an amended complaint.

Los Angeles City Attorney Hydee Feldstein Soto said the ruling affirmed that local governments can decide how to use their personnel and resources. She sa
articles.content:
A federal judge in California dismissed a Trump administration lawsuit against Los Angeles over a city ordinance that limits the city’s cooperation with federal immigration authorities. Judge Fernando Olguin rejected the administration’s argument that the policy was unconstitutional, while allowing the government to file an amended complaint.

Los Angeles City Attorney Hydee Feldstein Soto said the ruling affirmed that local governments can decide how to use their personnel and resources. She said the ordinance is intended to encourage crime victims and witnesses to seek help regardless of immigration status and does not block lawful federal enforcement operations.

The lawsuit, filed last June, argued that Los Angeles violated federal law by restricting the use of city resources to assist immigration enforcement or collect citizenship information. The administration has filed similar lawsuits against other Democratic-led cities, and federal judges have also dismissed cases against Boston and Chicago.

Andrade said he does not plan to get involved in the race and described his comments as part of an obligation to address what he view
articles.content:
State Rep. Alex Andrade, a Pensacola Republican, said he has not decided how he will vote in Florida’s November attorney general election, even as he continues to criticize Attorney General James Uthmeier. Andrade previously led an investigation into state funding tied to campaigns managed by Uthmeier and has continued to raise questions about Uthmeier’s conduct.

Andrade said he does not plan to get involved in the race and described his comments as part of an obligation to address what he views as corruption. Uthmeier secured the Republican nomination without opposition and will face Democrat José Javier Rodríguez in November.

A spokesperson for Uthmeier responded by pointing to the attorney general’s record on consumer recoveries, prosecutions, and endorsements. Andrade, who is term-limited and says he does not plan to seek office again, also questioned Uthmeier’s involvement in the University of Florida leadership dispute and his paid teaching role at UF.
